actualize.name: Actualize
actualize.short: The far rung of the ladder: feed findings back as self-referential claims a system queries before acting, so it resists the next failure - the single-agent seed of the group-scale failures evals study.
actualize.def: Actualize is the far rung of the specify-deduce-actualize ladder: feed findings back as self-referential claims a system queries before acting, so it resists the next failure. The single-agent self-knowledge it enables (reflexive agreement, treating speculation as ground truth) is continuous with the group-scale failures the eval vertical studies (conformity collapse, monoculture) - the same epistemic failure at two scales. It is a vision-horizon capability, presented as where the capability could go, never as shipped.

adjudication.name: Adjudication
adjudication.short: Applying a captured human decision to a conflict structurally - accept_supersede, reject_dep_tie, or defer - with a race guard and one atomic write.
adjudication.def: Adjudication (CB.Belief.Adjudication, adjudication.ex) applies a captured human decision to a conflict as a single atomic write, re-checking that the conflicting node is still active first (a race guard). The three outcomes are accept_supersede (write the successor and flip the loser to superseded), reject_dep_tie (write the proposal with a dep on the overlapping belief plus a rejection evidence entry), and defer (record a deferral primitive). It is the step that turns a surfaced preflight conflict into a recorded, structural outcome, never a silent overwrite.

ampliative.name: Ampliative
ampliative.short: The property of a conclusion that says more than its premises, the move every inference records and no compound is allowed to make.
ampliative.def: A move is ampliative when its conclusion outruns its premises - a generalization or an abduction whose content exceeds what was given (cb:a473, cb:a472). The ampliative move is exactly what an inference records and exactly what a compound is forbidden from making, since a conjunction can carry no content its parts lack. The worked illustration: two scorers each report one omitted record on one case, and the inference that the model silently drops records from all such bulk writes ever is the ampliative leap no chain of deductive steps can bridge.

anchor.name: Anchor
anchor.short: A literal substring matched per line (grep -nF semantics) to locate a source line at render time; resolved by CB.Anchor and never stored.
anchor.def: An anchor is a literal substring that CB.Anchor (anchor.ex) matches per line, with grep -nF semantics, to locate a source line at render time. Resolved line numbers are never stored; they are recomputed each time, so a missing file or anchor yields a warning rather than a crash (a maintenance signal), and multiple matches with no @N selector render the first with a tighten-this-anchor warning. The belief-free resolver is shared by codepaths and by draft-mode resolution.

anchored-position-format.name: Anchored-Position Format
anchored-position-format.short: The document shape for a position: a Class header, ### Claim: sections with optional **Anchor:** code: lines verified resolving at authoring time, with claim order as the default codepath walk.
anchored-position-format.def: The anchored-position format (cb:a550, cb:a551) is the document shape: a **Class:** header, then ### Claim: sections each with optional **Anchor:** lines holding code: URIs, verified resolving with exactly one match at authoring time via the draft-mode resolver. Claim order is the default codepath walk order, and an optional terms block carries {term, definition, anchor?}. It is what makes a position directly renderable as a tour.

answer-time-anchoring.name: Answer-Time Anchoring
answer-time-anchoring.short: The rule that agent claims about code must carry code: anchors that resolve at read time; an unresolvable anchor is an ungrounded claim to be withdrawn.
answer-time-anchoring.def: Answer-time anchoring (cb:a488, cb:a485) requires that agent claims about code carry code: anchors that resolve at read time, and an unresolvable anchor is an ungrounded claim. It rests on the broader point that prose discussion is productive exactly when every claim terminates in an anchor into the running substrate (cb:a485, cb:a484), and that reading code re-scopes attention to the anchored stops carrying an argument - the editor as an adjudication surface (cb:a486, cb:a487). Its verification gate is draft mode.

artifact.name: Artifact
artifact.short: A belief's provenance as a typed URI scheme:id naming the external referent it derives from; the scheme is drawn from the closed enum cb:c066.
artifact.def: The artifact field carries provenance as a typed URI scheme:id naming the external referent a belief was derived from (cb:a398, cb:a400, cb:c056). Every primitive cites one artifact; the scheme is drawn from the closed artifact-scheme enum cb:c066 and must match ^[a-z][a-z0-9_-]*:.+ (verifier.ex:237). It replaced the old quote/source pair, separating the typed referent from the dated evidence that grounds the claim. See provenance.

artifact-scheme.name: Artifact Scheme
artifact-scheme.short: The closed set of artifact URI prefixes (cb:c066): document, code, session, user, source, https, plan; collections may declare their own, like eval.
artifact-scheme.def: The artifact scheme is the lowercase token before the first colon of an artifact URI, drawn from the closed enum cb:c066: document: (a whole repo file), code: (an anchored within-file site), session:, user:, source: (a cached source doc), https:, and plan: (cb:a407). The code: scheme was the c040-to-c043 addition and c066 dropped the connector-specific gmail:. Adding a value supersedes the whole enum contract (cb:a397). Collections may declare their own schemes - the method: collection adds eval:. The subset that can ground a directive is the stipulation set.

assertions-gradient.name: Assertions Gradient
assertions-gradient.short: The single gradient of a codepath: contract-grade stops run predicates while non-contract stops narrate only, so the same artifact is both a guided walk and a test suite.
assertions-gradient.def: The assertions gradient is what makes a codepath one artifact with two faces: contract-grade stops run their routed predicates, while non-contract stops produce no rows and narrate only (codepath/assertions.ex:48). That asymmetry is the gradient - assertions off is a guided walk, assertions on is a test suite over real source. It lets a single anchored tour serve both teaching and regression checking.

atomic-write.name: Atomic Write
atomic-write.short: CB.JSON.write_atomic_raw: the tmp-write-then-rename pattern every writer bottoms out in; on failure it removes the tmp and leaves the original intact.
atomic-write.def: CB.JSON.write_atomic_raw/2 (json.ex:17) is the tmp-write-then-rename pattern that every writer in the system bottoms out in: it writes a temp file, then renames it over the target, and on failure removes the temp and leaves the original intact. Combined with the _keys round-trip, it lets the store rewrite the whole belief array on every write without corruption or churn. It is the durability guarantee under the single-file graph.

audit-tree.name: Audit Tree
audit-tree.short: The published artifact: a self-contained, zero-JavaScript HTML rendering of a verdict's full evidence tree down to the raw logs, with superseded nodes struck through and stale badges.
audit-tree.def: The audit tree is the published artifact (mix cb.render.audit, CB.Render.Audit): a self-contained, zero-JavaScript HTML rendering of a verdict's full evidence tree from the verdict at root down to leaf observations and their raw-log pointers, with superseded nodes struck through and stale badges on anything resting on superseded deps. A reader needs a browser, not Elixir. It is byte-stable for a given graph and id, with a sha256 digest in the footer.

author-against-live-graph.name: Author Against the Live Graph
author-against-live-graph.short: Pull the repos, query the desk, and follow artifacts before minting; a memory snapshot rots the moment another concurrent session pushes.
author-against-live-graph.def: The discipline is to author against the live graph, never from memory (cb:a504, cb:a508): pull the repos, query the desk, and follow artifacts before minting a belief, because multiple sessions write concurrently and a memory snapshot rots the moment another thread pushes. It is the operating-procedure consequence of treating session memory as an ephemeral cache, and the reason the recency view exists.

axiom-is-a-role.name: Axiom Is a Role
axiom-is-a-role.short: Entrenchment is earned through fan-in read off the dependents count, never declared as a type; an axiomatic node stays fully revisable.
axiom-is-a-role.def: CB has no axiom type: being foundational is a role a belief earns through fan-in, read off mix bs dependents, not a flag it declares (cb:a506). The most-depended-on nodes (cb:a467, cb:a470, cb:a485 and a few others) are functionally axiomatic by how much rests on them, yet they remain ordinary revisable beliefs subject to the same write flow. This keeps the graph free of privileged nodes that escape supersession, consistent with the no maintenance hatch principle.

belief.name: Belief
belief.short: One node of the graph: a single atomic claim with a type, kind, domain, claim text, provenance or deps, subjects, and lifecycle status.
belief.def: A belief is the unit node of the DAG: one structured %CB.Belief{} claim about the world carrying a structural type, a kind, a domain, a one-line claim, provenance or deps, subjects, and a status (cb:a478, lib/cb/belief.ex). Beliefs are immutable: a change means a new node, never an in-place edit. In memory the whole graph is a flat list of these structs with deps as the only edges.

belief-shell.name: Belief Shell (bs)
belief-shell.short: The deterministic, LLM-free query CLI over the graph: mix bs with subcommands list, show, tree, deps, dependents, stale, recent, path, history, subjects, stats.
belief-shell.def: The belief shell (mix bs, Mix.Tasks.Bs, bs.ex) is the deterministic, LLM-free query CLI over the graph. Its read-only subcommands include list (with type, status, tag, kind, domain, subject, stale, contracts, and unlinked filters), show, tree, deps, dependents, stale, recent, path, history, subjects, and stats. It is how a session queries the desk and walks supersession chains. See the belief shell.

beliefs-vs-contracts.name: Beliefs vs Contracts
beliefs-vs-contracts.short: Two roles sharing one store: a belief says this is true and can become stale; a contract says this will remain true and gets violated or upheld.
beliefs-vs-contracts.def: Beliefs and contracts are two roles sharing one store (cb:a120): a belief says this is true, while a contract says this will remain true. The consequence is different lifecycles - a belief becomes stale and gets superseded, while a contract gets violated or upheld. Because both live in the same graph with the same schema, a behavioral invariant can start life as a prompt-enforced directive and be elevated to a code-enforced contract once code crystallizes around it - inferences are prototypes for contracts.

build-order-ring.name: Build-Order Ring
build-order-ring.short: Resourcing language, not architecture: a capability is exercised when a real use pulls it; Ring 0 is now, Ring 1 is demand-pulled now, Rings 2 and 2+ are the vision horizon.
build-order-ring.def: A ring is build-order, not architecture: a capability is exercised when a real use pulls it, so the rings describe founder-hours, not CB's identity (which is architecturally upstream of all of them). Ring 0 is the receptive ledger and belief composition (now); Ring 1 is contract interpreters and compile-to-tests (demand-pulled now, the eval-spec-as-contract); Rings 2 and 2+ are deduced remediation and self-reference (the vision horizon). Discipline, not hype: only Ring 1 touches v1.

c-prefix.name: c-Prefix / a-Prefix
c-prefix.short: The id-grade convention: c-prefixed local ids are reserved for contracts and a-prefixed for everything else, enforced in both directions.
c-prefix.def: Local id prefixes encode grade by convention: c### is reserved for contract-grade beliefs and a### for everything else (adjudication.ex, verifier.ex:586). The verifier enforces that every c-prefixed local id carries contract: true, and id generation stamps the right prefix on new nodes. The prefix is convention only and never the identity test: contract status is detected structurally via CB.Belief.contract?/1 (cb:c054), so a contract is known by its non-empty rules or invariants, not its id.

cb-holds-what-how.name: cb: Holds Only What and How
cb-holds-what-how.short: The framework collection holds schema, mechanism, and positioning; the motivating why - agent failure modes, the paradigm argument - lives in agent-behavior: and paradigm:.
cb-holds-what-how.def: The cb: collection holds only the framework's what and how - schema, mechanism, positioning - while the motivating why lives elsewhere: agent failure modes in agent-behavior: and the paradigm argument in paradigm: (cb:a464, cb:a492). Because cb: depends only within itself, the framework ships clean and self-contained, without the narrative that motivated it. This is the collection-level expression of keeping the substrate consumer-blind.

cb-is-ledger-not-bench.name: CB Is the Ledger, Not the Bench
cb-is-ledger-not-bench.short: Eval execution stays in an external harness; CB ingests output records and never grows toward execution, keeping its deterministic LLM-free read path intact.
cb-is-ledger-not-bench.def: CB is the ledger, not the lab bench (docs/eval-ledger.md, cb:a539): eval execution stays in an external harness, and CB ingests the run-manifest output records and never grows toward execution. The division of labor is enforced by the tool's shape - import.eval emits observation primitives only. Refusing to absorb the bench keeps CB's deterministic, LLM-free read path intact, the same boundary as reasoning substrate, not memory.

centralized-graph.name: Centralized Single-File Graph
centralized-graph.short: One beliefs.json is preferred over per-entity belief files, so the whole graph is queryable, diffable, and atomically writable as a single artifact.
centralized-graph.def: A centralized single-file graph is preferred over per-entity belief files (cb:a112): the whole collection lives in one beliefs.json, which keeps it queryable as a unit, diffable in git, and atomically writable. The _keys round-trip is what makes rewriting the whole array on every write practical. It is the storage decision underneath the store boundary.

ceremony-proportionate.name: Ceremony Proportionate to Stance
ceremony-proportionate.short: Persistence ceremony scales with the weight of the stance: a position earns its keep when the reasoning is the artifact; an obvious prescription goes straight in as a directive.
ceremony-proportionate.def: Persistence ceremony is proportionate to stance (cb:a496): a position document earns its keep when the reasoning itself is the artifact, while an observation with an obvious prescription skips the document and goes straight in as a directive. The justification is practical - if every wart became a three-artifact pipeline, the pipeline would stop being read. It is the same proportionality that governs which sessions persist verbatim.

chronicle.name: Chronicle
chronicle.short: A dated prose narrative for the operator - where things stood and stand, story beats, what the next session inherits - in which narrative carries the load and ids stay subordinate.
chronicle.def: A chronicle (cb:a520) is a dated prose narrative for the operator, living in chronicles/: where things stood and stand, the story beats, and what the next session inherits. The division of labor is that the transcript serves the audit, the graph serves the work, and the chronicle serves the steering. In a chronicle narrative carries the load and ids stay subordinate, the inverse of the receipts register a transcript is.

claim.name: Claim
claim.short: The one-line proposition a belief asserts; the only field that survives into compiled output, so multi-paragraph analyses live as source docs, not claims.
claim.def: The claim is the atomic one-liner a belief asserts, and it is the only field that survives into compiled output rendering (output_target.ex:131). Stuffing a multi-paragraph analysis into a claim inverts the relationship - the prose becomes the point and the claim becomes a title - so analyses live as source documents citing belief ids inline (cb:a114). For descriptive beliefs the claim is the generalization the author is responsible for; the gap between it and the evidence is where misinterpretation can be audited.

claim-evidence-gap.name: Claim / Evidence Gap
claim-evidence-gap.short: The audited distance between a belief's generalization (the claim) and the factual narrative under it (the evidence), where misinterpretation lives.
claim-evidence-gap.def: The claim/evidence gap is the deliberate separation between the claim (the agent's interpretation, a generalization) and the evidence detail (the factual narrative of what the source actually said) (cb:a118, cb:a124). The gap is where misinterpretation lives, so it is made inspectable: a future session can ask whether the claim actually follows from these words. Keeping the two in distinct fields is what makes provenance auditable rather than asserted.

code-locator.name: Code Locator
code-locator.short: The parser for code: artifact URIs of the form code:<path>#<anchor>[@N]; the single parser the verifier and renderer share, storing no line numbers.
code-locator.def: The code locator (CB.CodeLocator, code_locator.ex) parses a code: artifact: the path runs to the first #, everything after is one opaque literal-substring anchor (which may itself contain #), and an optional trailing @N selects the Nth match (a literal trailing @digits is percent-encoded as %40digits). It stores no line numbers; resolution happens at read time so refactors do not break the path (cb:a467). It is the single parser the verifier and renderer share.

code-scheme.name: code: Scheme
code-scheme.short: The artifact scheme code:<path>#<anchor>[@N] naming an anchored site in a source file; line numbers are never stored, so refactors do not break the reference.
code-scheme.def: The code: scheme names an anchored site in a source file: code:<repo-relative-path>#<anchor>[@N] (cb:a467, parsed by CB.CodeLocator). Everything after the first # is one opaque literal-substring anchor, with an optional @N occurrence selector. Line numbers are never stored; they are resolved at render or run time by fixed-string match, so refactors do not break the path (cb:c066). It is the scheme behind answer-time anchoring and codepaths.

codepath.name: Codepath
codepath.short: A code-anchored belief collection that reads as a narrated, branching tour of real source and runs as a test suite over it - one artifact, one gradient.
codepath.def: A codepath is a code-anchored belief collection that is simultaneously a narrated, branching tour of real source and a runnable test suite over it - same artifact, one gradient: assertions off is a guided walk, assertions on means contract-grade stops execute their routed predicates (cb:a467, CB.Codepath, codepath.ex). Each role has one home: location is the code: artifact, narration is the claim, derivation is the deps, assertion is the implies rules, and order is a separate render-spec. It extends CB's signature property - traverse from a verdict to the exact raw run - one level deeper, into the code. See codepaths.

codepath-render-spec.name: Codepath Render-Spec
codepath-render-spec.short: An output-target contract tagged output:codepath with an entry step and render_steps rows {id, belief, goto?, choices?}; navigation is render metadata, never deps or claims.
codepath-render-spec.def: The codepath render-spec (cb:c049) is an output-target contract tagged output:codepath, carrying an entry step id and render_steps rows of shape {id, belief, goto?, choices?} (output_target.ex). Navigation is render metadata that never enters deps and never lives in claims, so reordering supersedes the render-spec rather than the claims, and the spec's deps equal the union of the rows' belief ids. Traversal is depth-first from entry, visited-guarded so cycles terminate.

collection.name: Collection
collection.short: A beliefs.json graph in one declared namespace, with a sibling manifest.json carrying its namespace, description, and cross-namespace depends_on.
collection.def: A collection is a beliefs.json graph in a declared namespace, with a sibling manifest.json carrying namespace, description, and cross-namespace depends_on (cb:a463, CB.Collection, collection.ex). Any collection is queryable with mix bs --beliefs PATH or CB_BELIEFS. The live map of every collection is collections.json; examples include cb, agent-behavior, method, sdl, and toy. See collections and namespaces.

collections-registry.name: Collections Registry
collections-registry.short: collections.json: the namespace-to-path map against which dependency closures resolve, so mix cb.verify.collection can find every depended-on graph.
collections-registry.def: The collections registry is collections.json, mapping each namespace to the relative path of its beliefs.json (CB.Collection.Registry, collection.ex:34). Dependency closures resolve against it, so mix cb.verify.collection can find every depended-on graph. It is the local SSOT for which collections exist and where they live (cb:a464), the live map prose files route to rather than enumerate.

compiled-document.name: Compiled Document
compiled-document.short: A document like CLAUDE.md compiled from beliefs listed in an output target's render_sections; read-only, every line traces to one claim, and CI-gated against the graph.
compiled-document.def: A compiled document is generated from the graph: CLAUDE.md compiles from the beliefs an output target names in render_sections (cb:c060, cb:c065, cb:a466, cb:a482), so it is read-only, every line traces to one belief claim, and CI gates the committed file against the graph. Compiled output also carries the source belief ids for traceability (cb:a517). Rendering live and gating in CI is the structural answer to the digest antipattern.

compiled-vs-rendered.name: Compiled Face vs Rendered Face
compiled-vs-rendered.short: Compiled documents face the agent (CLAUDE.md, rule files); rendered trees and tours face the human (audit trees, codepaths); both derive live from the graph.
compiled-vs-rendered.def: The graph has two output faces: compiled documents face the agent (CLAUDE.md, rule files), while rendered trees and tours face the human (the audit tree, the codepath). Both derive live from the graph rather than from a hand-maintained copy. It is the same split named at the artifact level by position versus codepath.

composable-beliefs.name: Composable Beliefs
composable-beliefs.short: The paradigm: a DAG of source-grounded, immutable, composable claims that gives agents persistent, inspectable reasoning across session boundaries.
composable-beliefs.def: Composable Beliefs (CB) is a framework for storing reasoning as a directed acyclic graph of typed, immutable, source-grounded claims, so that what one session concludes is readable and auditable by every later session and subagent without re-derivation (thesis; cb:a478). It is a pure deterministic graph over one JSON file (lib/cb/belief.ex), with an LLM-free read path so trust comes from structure and cited evidence. The framework ships as the cb: collection and is exercised as a reasoning and audit substrate by consumers such as amieval. See belief and structural type.

composition-over-retrieval.name: Composition Over Retrieval
composition-over-retrieval.short: The DAG's value is concluding what follows from combining facts, surfacing questions no single document contains, rather than finding known context the way RAG does.
composition-over-retrieval.def: Composition over retrieval (cb:a462, cb:a539) names where the DAG's value lies: concluding what follows from combining facts, and surfacing questions no single document contains, rather than finding known context the way retrieval-augmented generation does. RAG finds known answers; this graph's payoff is the inference a single document cannot hold. It is the reason CB stays a reasoning substrate and refuses to grow into a recall system.

compound.name: Compound
compound.short: Aggregate: a conjunction stating exactly what its two-or-more deps jointly state, with subjects contained in the union of its deps' subjects.
compound.def: A compound records the operation aggregate: a conjunction whose claim states exactly what its deps (two or more) jointly state, no more (cb:c051, cb:a474, the strict-aggregate doctrine). It is the one deduction the schema stores (cb:a473): A and B, therefore A-and-B is truth-preserving and non-ampliative, and its epistemic work is the selection of what to assemble and the naming of the agreement. Subject containment (cb:c058) is its formal shadow: a compound's subject refs must be a subset of the union of its deps' subject refs. The framework's own cb: graph holds no compounds; they are exercised in eval collections as cross-ruler agreement.

conflict-audit.name: Conflict Audit
conflict-audit.short: The pass (mix cb.audit.conflicts) that surfaces overlapping-directive pairs and stale overrides as candidates for review; overlap is a candidate, never a verdict.
conflict-audit.def: The conflict audit (CB.Audit.Conflicts, audit/conflicts.ex) surfaces two things for review: stale overrides (active nodes depping on superseded or retracted nodes) and scope overlaps (pairs of active directives sharing conflict scope). An overlap is a candidate for human judgment, never a verdict - semantic contradiction needs a reviewer (cb:c055). It is exposed as mix cb.audit.conflicts with filters for tag, domain, and a single related node.

conflict-scope.name: Conflict Scope
conflict-scope.short: When two active directives overlap on a shared tag, subject ref, or subject type within one domain; necessary but not sufficient for contradiction.
conflict-scope.def: Two active directives are in conflict scope when they overlap on at least one axis - a shared tag, subject ref, or subject type - within the same domain (cb:c055, supersedes cb:c032). Overlap is symmetric, never reflexive, and never cross-domain, and it is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction. Contradictory inferences are dissent, out of this audit's scope (consensus-thread work). The pressure behind requiring adjudication is that contradictions in the governance substrate must be expensive to introduce (cb:a304).

consensus-mechanism.name: Consensus Mechanism
consensus-mechanism.short: The design root of the write flow: surface conflicts at authoring time, route resolution through adjudication, and produce a recorded outcome, so authoring does not depend on agent memory.
consensus-mechanism.def: The consensus mechanism (cb:a387, cb:a304) is the design root behind the write flow: surface conflicts at authoring time, route resolution through adjudication (human, multi-agent, or tiered by conflict weight), and produce a recorded outcome. Without it the authoring surface would depend on agent memory, which rots as concurrent threads push. Open extensions are consensual primitives (per-source attestations plus a consensus compound, cb:a494) and multi-agent reconciliation (cb:a495). It is why contradictions in the governance substrate are expensive to introduce.

contract.name: Contract
contract.short: A directive formalized to machine-checkable grade: it has non-empty rules or invariants; a structural grade of a directive, never a fourth type.
contract.def: A contract is a directive formalized to machine-checkable grade, carrying contract: true with non-empty rules or invariants (cb:c054, founding def cb:a300). Where an inference states WHAT (the conclusion), a contract states HOW (rules as Given/When/Then) and ALWAYS (invariants). Contract is a structural grade of a directive, never a fourth type, and it is detected structurally via CB.Belief.contract?/1, with the c-prefix being convention only (belief.ex:118). See contracts.

contract-interpreter.name: Contract Interpreter
contract-interpreter.short: One generic Elixir module per contract kind (Enum, StateMachine, Table, Implies) that reads a contract's rules as a tiny Datalog relation; the belief is passed in, no ids hardcoded.
contract-interpreter.def: A contract interpreter is one generic Elixir module per contract kind that reads a contract's rules as a tiny Datalog relation: CB.Belief.Contract.Enum, .StateMachine, .Table, and .Implies (lib/cb/belief/contract/*). Each is generic, taking the belief in with no hardcoded ids, which is what lets the same code interpret any collection's contracts. The interpreters embody the implementation half of the routing/implementation boundary.

contracts-prose-code-gap.name: Neither Prose nor Code
contracts-prose-code-gap.short: Contracts are the intermediate level of formality: a prose rule cannot be mechanically checked, code cannot carry its own rationale, and a contract does both.
contracts-prose-code-gap.def: Contracts fill the gap between literal code and plain English (cb:a133, cb:a134, cb:a135, synthesized in cb:a138): a prose rule cannot be mechanically checked, and code cannot carry its own rationale, provenance, and supersession history, while a contract does both. Contracts are domain-bound (they survive code rewrites), substrate-independent (one state-machine contract is implemented in both compiled code and agent reasoning, cb:a135), and they generate tests rather than the reverse (cb:a134). This intermediate formality is what makes it safe for the graph to drive tests.

cross-collection-desk.name: Cross-Collection Desk (Future)
cross-collection-desk.short: A proposed single query surface for unmaterialized discrete directives across every registered collection, plus a global directive graph in dotfiles.
cross-collection-desk.def: The cross-collection desk (cb:a500, cb:a543) is an open extension: one query surface for unmaterialized discrete directives across all registered collections, rather than per-collection, with a global directive graph living in dotfiles. It generalizes the desk from a single graph to the whole working tree, so an operator can see every obligation in one place.

cross-ruler-agreement.name: Cross-Ruler Agreement
cross-ruler-agreement.short: A compound asserting that two independent rulers reached the same outcome on the same case - a claim neither observation makes alone; required under a verdict by method:c4.
cross-ruler-agreement.def: Cross-ruler agreement is the eval compound: it asserts that two independent rulers reached the same outcome on the same case, corroboration neither observation states alone yet fully contained in what they say together (eval-ledger.md). It is human-authored, and contract method:c4 (m-corroboration) requires every verdict to reach such a compound or carry a single-ruler tag. It is the worked example of the strict-aggregate doctrine outside the framework's own graph.

dag-authorized-migration.name: DAG-Authorized Migration
dag-authorized-migration.short: Field removal or rename happens only through an adjudicated migration anchored by a compound; it is the single mutation class the schema allows on its own shape.
dag-authorized-migration.def: Schema evolution - removing or renaming a field - happens only through an adjudicated migration that the graph itself authorizes, anchored by a compound recording the decision (cb:a436, cb:a438). This is the one mutation class allowed on the schema's own shape, and it is why the expunged fields are gone by record rather than by silent deletion. It is the schema-evolution instance of the no maintenance hatch principle: even the shape of a belief changes only through the write flow.

dag-vs-prose.name: DAG-vs-Prose Eval
dag-vs-prose.short: The study comparing a structured CB graph against a prose baseline on agent reasoning tasks; the OKF/Karpathy wiki is the credible prose arm the structured arm must beat.
dag-vs-prose.def: The dag-vs-prose eval is the study comparing a structured CB graph against a prose baseline on agent reasoning and adherence tasks (bench/dag-vs-prose-v2). Real runs exist - one DAG context scored 9/9 compound adherence against prose at 2/9. The credible prose arm is an OKF/Karpathy agent-maintained wiki rather than a strawman (cb:a544), which raises the bar the structured arm must clear and locates CB's remaining margin in the typed, deterministic, self-referential parts OKF omits. It is the empirical test behind the framework's central claim.

decision-weight-session.name: Decision-Weight Session
decision-weight-session.short: A session that mints or supersedes grounded beliefs, settles a stance, or adjudicates positions; it persists its thread verbatim, routed by subject.
decision-weight-session.def: A decision-weight session (cb:a540) is one that mints or supersedes beliefs grounded in a user: or session: stipulation, settles a stance, or adjudicates positions. Such a session persists its thread verbatim, routed by subject - direction threads to the private direction repos, plan-scale dev to co-located plans/. Every belief minted is retro-paired with a document pointer. Lighter sessions need no verbatim persistence, by ceremony proportionate to stance.

dep-repoint.name: Dep-Repoint Front Door
dep-repoint.short: mix cb.repoint: a sanctioned, atomic task to swing a dep from a superseded node to its successor (drop plus add).
dep-repoint.def: The dep-repoint front door (mix cb.repoint, cb:a537, cb:a547) swings a dep from a superseded node to its successor as an atomic drop-plus-add (cb.repoint.ex). It is the maintenance move that clears staleness caused by a dep being superseded, and the third of the three sanctioned front doors. Some of its automation is still an open gap.

dependency-closure.name: Dependency Closure / Union
dependency-closure.short: The transitive, cycle-safe set of namespaces a collection needs, loaded as one flat belief list for verification and rendering.
dependency-closure.def: The dependency closure is the transitive, cycle-safe set of namespaces a collection needs, resolved target-first through the registry and loaded as one flat belief list - the union (CB.Collection.closure and load_union, collection.ex:121). Verification and rendering of a non-leaf collection run over that union, so mix cb.verify.collection <ns> checks a collection together with everything it borrows. The union is how a methodology contract in method: can check beliefs in sdl:.

depends-on.name: depends_on / Borrowing by Role
depends-on.short: A collection declares which namespaces it depends on in its manifest; it borrows their contracts by role, not by id, so the verifier discovers each by what it declares.
depends-on.def: A collection declares its cross-namespace dependencies in manifest.json via depends_on (collection.ex:102). It borrows the depended-on collection's contracts by role, not by id - the verifier discovers an enum by the field it declares and the lifecycle by its tag - so a borrower picks up cb: schema vocabulary without naming specific contract ids. A field for which no contract is borrowed is skipped, not failed.

deprecation.name: Deprecation
deprecation.short: A pause rather than a retirement - not currently useful enough to carry - with the path back open; the reference case is the dropped confidence field.
deprecation.def: Deprecation marks a belief or feature as not currently useful enough to carry, rather than wrong forever, and reinstatement is itself a belief (cb:a390). The reference case is the confidence field, deprecated rather than deleted so the path back to a derived confidence stays open if a concrete use-case ever appears. Deprecation contrasts with retraction, which asserts that something is wrong.

deps.name: Deps
deps.short: Belief-to-belief logical derivation: the upstream ids whose claims jointly justify this claim; the DAG's only edges, absent on primitives.
deps.def: deps is the belief-to-belief relation of logical derivation - the upstream ids whose claims together justify this claim - and it is the only kind of edge the DAG has (cb:a408, belief.ex:51, graph.ex:41). Deps are required on compounds and inferences, required-or-stipulated on non-contract directives, and absent on primitives. They are distinct from subjects: a belief can be about something without depending on it, and depend on something without being about it. Edges are implicit string pointers resolved on demand by CB.Belief.Graph.

derivation-table.name: derivation-table
derivation-table.short: A contract kind whose rules are arbitrary-column rows, looked up by ANDing conditions; the kind behind the kind-type table.
derivation-table.def: derivation-table is a catalogued contract kind (cb:c046): its rules are arbitrary-column maps yielding row(Col1..ColN), looked up by ANDing conditions, read by CB.Belief.Contract.Table (table.ex). It is the kind behind the kind-type table (cb:c057), which maps each kind to its allowed structural types. Like every interpreter it is generic over the belief passed in, with no hardcoded ids.

digest-antipattern.name: Digest-File Antipattern
digest-antipattern.short: A persisted cache of graph-derived content whose freshness is procedural embeds the very staleness it was meant to solve; the fix is to render live from the graph and gate in CI.
digest-antipattern.def: The digest-file antipattern (cb:a386, the canonical example in mental-model.md) is a persisted cache of graph-derived content whose freshness depends on procedural enforcement - a skill telling agents to regenerate - which embeds the very staleness it was meant to solve, because agents forget and the stale digest is then read as authoritative. The elimination path generalizes: render live from the DAG and gate in CI (cb:a466, cb:a477). The same doctrine governs compiled documents and session memory. See staleness.

direction-of-fit.name: Direction of Fit
direction-of-fit.short: The operational boundary between the two derived types: inference is mind-fits-world (falsified); directive is world-fits-mind (violated or withdrawn).
direction-of-fit.def: Direction of fit is the boundary between inference and directive made operational - ask what would count as the belief being wrong (cb:a471, mental-model.md). An inference has mind-fits-world fit: the world disagrees, so the belief is defective and is superseded or retracted. A directive has world-fits-mind fit: the world disobeys, so the violation is flagged and the rule stands, or the house withdraws and supersedes or retires it. The author-facing trichotomy is falsified to inference, violated to directive, withdrawn to directive, and the boundary is enforced because materialization, the conflict audit, and contract grading attach only to directives (cb:a470).

directive.name: Directive
directive.short: Prescribe: something that should happen or must hold (rule, guidance, policy, contract); violated or withdrawn, never falsified.
directive.def: A directive records the operation prescribe: something that should happen or must hold - a rule, guidance, policy, or contract (cb:c051, cb:a471). A directive is the only type that can be materialized, conflict-audited, or graded into a contract; it is grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact (cb:c059). By direction of fit a directive is violated (the world disobeys, so flag the violation and keep the rule) or withdrawn (the house stops standing behind it, so supersede or retire it), and is never falsified. In eval work guidance is the directive face; it is the structural type that carries work, the desk.

directive-grounding.name: Directive Grounding
directive-grounding.short: The rule (cb:c059) that an active non-contract directive grounds in deps or a stipulation artifact (plan, user, session, document); external-source schemes never ground a directive.
directive-grounding.def: Directive grounding (cb:c059, cb:a476) requires that an active non-contract directive rest on deps or a stipulation artifact whose scheme is plan, user, session, or document (verifier.ex:373). External-source schemes like source and https never ground a directive, because a rule the house enforces must trace to an adoption event, not merely to an outside reference. This is the prescriptive counterpart to a primitive's single-artifact grounding.

discovered-by-role.name: Discovered by Role
discovered-by-role.short: The verifier finds a collection's contracts by what they declare, not by id: an enum by the field it governs, the lifecycle by its tag, the kind-type table by its columns.
discovered-by-role.def: The verifier discovers a collection's vocabulary by role rather than by hardcoded id: an enum contract is found by the field it declares, the status state machine by its status-lifecycle tag, the kind-type table by its columns (verifier.ex:77). This is what lets a downstream collection borrow contracts by role through depends_on, and what makes the same engine verify the framework's own graph and every borrower's. A field with no governing contract is skipped, not failed.

domain.name: Domain
domain.short: A belief's topical area, the closed five-value enum cb:c041: system, design, agent, ops, dev.
domain.def: domain places a belief in a topical area, the closed enum cb:c041: system (system and process design), design, agent (agent behavior, capability, limitation), ops (operational and business workflows), and dev (agent-system development and infra) (belief.ex:51). Domain matters for conflict scope: two active directives overlap only within the same domain, and distinct non-null domains never overlap (cb:c055).

draft-mode.name: Draft Mode
draft-mode.short: Anchor resolution with no belief collection loaded (mix cb.resolve), validating bare {path, anchor, nth} rows; the verification gate for answer-time anchoring.
draft-mode.def: Draft mode (mix cb.resolve, cb:a512) validates and resolves bare {path, anchor, nth} stop rows against a root with no belief collection loaded, so draft answers and anchored positions can use the one tested resolver. It is the verification gate for answer-time anchoring: landing cb:a512 unblocked cb:a488's escalation to contract grade. Exit 1 on any unresolved row makes it usable in CI.

drift-audit.name: Drift Audit
drift-audit.short: A first-class maintenance cycle iterating materialized directives, re-checking effects and bumping last_verified or emitting a drift primitive when effects have decayed.
drift-audit.def: A drift audit (cb:a384) is the maintenance cycle that iterates non-null materialized beliefs, re-checks their effects, and either bumps last_verified or emits a drift primitive. It exists because materialization records a historical execution, not a standing guarantee - effects decay as code drifts. It is the action-history counterpart to staleness detection on the truth axis.

dual-kinds.name: Dual Kinds
dual-kinds.short: The two kinds (definition, schema) that span both moods and may take any of the four structural types, decided per belief by direction of fit.
dual-kinds.def: Two kinds carry both moods and so map to all four structural types in the kind-type table: definition (vocabulary, not commitment - what a word, type, or field means, cb:a437, cb:a408) and schema (a rule about belief or DAG structure, cb:a300). For a dual-kind belief the structural type is decided per belief by direction of fit: a definition stated as a description is a primitive, while a schema rule the house enforces is a directive. The other prescriptive kinds, such as policy (an intentional commitment not mechanically enforced, cb:a439) and action-item (a single completable task, cb:a403), are directive only.

dynamic-verifier.name: Dynamic Verifier
dynamic-verifier.short: mix cb.verify.codepath: the one place codepath predicates actually run; --record writes pass/fail back to the belief's materialized field.
dynamic-verifier.def: The dynamic verifier (mix cb.verify.codepath, CB.Codepath.Assertions, codepath/assertions.ex) is the one place codepath predicates actually execute (cb:c050). For each contract-grade stop it takes the implies rules applicable under assertions: on and invokes each predicate, and --record writes the pass/fail result back to the belief's materialized field as a recorded test run. It is the dynamic counterpart to the static method check pass.

enum-registry.name: enum-registry
enum-registry.short: A contract kind whose rules declare a closed set of allowed values for a field; the catalogued kind behind the kind, domain, and artifact-scheme enums.
enum-registry.def: enum-registry is one of the catalogued contract kinds (cb:c046): its rules declare {field, values}, yielding the fact allowed(Field, Value) read by CB.Belief.Contract.Enum (enum.ex). It is the kind behind the closed kind enum (cb:c039), the domain enum (cb:c041), and the artifact-scheme enum (cb:c066). Enum-shaped fields take values only from these dedicated contracts, and adding a value supersedes the whole enum contract (cb:a397), an instance of immutability applied to the schema itself.

eval-collection.name: Eval Collection
eval-collection.short: A namespaced belief collection holding one operation's eval graph, declaring depends_on: [method] to borrow the shared vocabulary and the six methodology contracts.
eval-collection.def: An eval collection is a namespaced collection holding one operation's eval graph, declaring depends_on: [method] to borrow the shared eval vocabulary and the six methodology contracts. The worked examples are sdl (eval-provenance, deliberately non-compliant for teaching) and toy (the fully compliant counterpart). The base method: collection holds the shared vocabulary every eval collection depends on. See the worked example.

eval-ledger.name: Eval Ledger
eval-ledger.short: The published evidence trail behind an eval finding, rendered as graph structure: every measurement a belief, every methodology rule a contract, every correction a visible supersession.
eval-ledger.def: The eval evidence ledger is the published trail behind a finding, rendered as graph structure: every measurement is a belief, every methodological rule a contract, and every correction a supersession a reader can see (docs/eval-ledger.md). A finding exercises all four structural types - observation, cross-ruler agreement, verdict, guidance - so a reader can traverse from a published verdict back to the exact raw run. It is amieval using CB's receptive face. See grounding eval verdicts.

eval-scheme.name: eval: Scheme
eval-scheme.short: A scorer-run identity URI declared by the method: collection (not by cb:), of the form eval:<eval_id>/<run_id>/<ruler>[/<case_id>].
eval-scheme.def: The eval: scheme is a scorer-run identity URI declared by the method: collection rather than by cb: itself, demonstrating that collections may extend the closed artifact-scheme enum for their own domain (eval-ledger.md, run-manifest.md). Its shape is eval:<eval_id>/<run_id>/<ruler> for an aggregate observation and adds /<case_id> for a per-case one. Contract method:c5 (m-provenance) refuses any observation lacking an eval: URI and a raw-log pointer.

eval-spec-as-contract.name: Eval-Spec as a Contract
eval-spec-as-contract.short: The hinge of the generative face: a pre-registered eval rubric is a contract (guarantees / requires / violates_if) that compiles to the runnable checks and to the writeup.
eval-spec-as-contract.def: The eval-spec-as-contract is the hinge that fuses the framework and the integration: a pre-registered eval rubric is itself a contract (with clauses like guarantees, requires, violates_if) that compiles to the runnable checks and to the writeup. The same compile step is what would later produce a client's guardrails - one mechanism, two products. It is the only generative slice the first eval needs, because a v1 eval wants a pre-registered machine-checkable spec anyway. It is the specify rung made concrete.

evidence.name: Evidence
evidence.short: Dated entries of shape {date, artifact, detail} that ground a claim; appending one is the single sanctioned in-place growth point on an immutable node.
evidence.def: evidence is a list of dated entries, each {date, artifact, detail}, grounding a belief's claim (cb:a118). The detail is the specific narrative of what happened or what the source said, while the claim is the generalization, and the gap between them is where misinterpretation lives and can be audited. Appending an evidence entry is the one sanctioned in-place growth point on an otherwise immutable node (cb:a302, cb:a522), exposed as the evidence-append front door. Evidence-entry artifacts are not enum-checked, so they may carry provenance schemes like adjudication:.

evidence-append.name: Evidence-Append Front Door
evidence-append.short: mix cb.evidence: the one sanctioned in-place growth point on an immutable node, appending a dated evidence entry.
evidence-append.def: The evidence-append front door (mix cb.evidence, cb:a522, cb:a531) appends a dated evidence entry to an existing belief - the single sanctioned in-place growth point on an otherwise immutable node. It is one of three narrow front doors named in the compiled CLAUDE.md surface, alongside todo close and dep re-point. Appending evidence grows the grounding of a claim without changing the claim itself.

expunged-fields.name: Expunged Fields
expunged-fields.short: Three fields that no longer exist - confidence, source, implication - stripped on load so legacy data round-trips clean; meanings carried elsewhere now.
expunged-fields.def: Three fields were removed from the schema and no longer exist: confidence (a subjective scalar replaced by structural support), source (renamed to the structured artifact), and implication (deleted, its meaning carried by claim plus deps) (belief.ex:194). from_map/1 strips these from _keys so legacy data round-trips clean, and the verifier flags any belief still carrying an implication key. Each removal happened through an adjudicated DAG-authorized migration, never a silent edit.

extraction-time-discipline.name: Extraction-Time Discipline
extraction-time-discipline.short: Provenance and beliefs are created when reading sources, not attributed retroactively; a source is raw material from which primitives are extracted.
extraction-time-discipline.def: Extraction-time discipline (cb:a119, cb:a125, cb:a131) requires that provenance and beliefs be created when reading sources, with retroactive attribution prohibited. A source is raw material from which primitives are extracted (cb:a113, cb:a114), so the graph stores structured beliefs rather than the sources themselves. Field-level _provenance on entities makes the data model auditable, and user-sourced provenance is authoritative (cb:a124, cb:a428, cb:a362).

falsifiability.name: Falsifiability
falsifiability.short: The price an inference pays for its licence: it can be shown wrong on its own while its deps stay true, triggering supersession.
falsifiability.def: Falsifiability is the obligation an inference accepts in exchange for its licence to generalize: the world can disagree with it directly, rendering it defective and triggering supersession or retraction, while its deps remain active (cb:a473, cb:a471). The operational test for the inference-versus-directive boundary is to ask what would count as the belief being wrong: if new evidence could show it false, it is an inference. In eval work the staleness pivot is the falsification mechanism - a re-run on a new model_version falsifies the verdict.

field-presence.name: Field Presence by Type
field-presence.short: The contract cb:c052 governing which fields each structural type requires: compounds and inferences need deps, non-contract directives need deps or a stipulation, contracts may stand alone.
field-presence.def: Field presence (cb:c052, supersedes cb:c027) is the contract governing which fields each structural type requires: compounds and inferences require deps; non-contract directives require deps or a stipulation artifact; declared contracts may stand alone; and contract fields are directive-only. It is the schema rule that makes the type-as-function relationship enforceable, checked in CB.Schema.Verifier check_grounding (verifier.ex:375).

fixture-tag.name: Fixture Tag
fixture-tag.short: The fixture tag (method:a5) auto-applied to anything derived from synthetic or mock data, so test scaffolding can never be dressed up as a real finding.
fixture-tag.def: The fixture tag (method:a5) is auto-applied to any belief derived from synthetic or mock data, so test scaffolding can never be mistaken for a finding. In the bench, mock-LLM data is force-tagged fixture at the source, and the compliant toy collection carries fixture-tagged guidance. It is a small integrity guard that keeps the ledger honest about what is real.

frontmatter.name: Frontmatter
frontmatter.short: The YAML-subset header block of an OKF doc, parsed by a hand-written parser that handles scalars, inline lists, and folded blocks (not full YAML).
frontmatter.def: Frontmatter is the YAML-subset header block of an OKF doc, parsed by the hand-written CB.Okf.Frontmatter (frontmatter.ex) which handles scalars, inline [a,b] lists, folded blocks, and block lists, and is deliberately not full YAML. It carries an OKF doc's type, status, id, and the tier marker. It is the per-document metadata that makes an OKF bundle self-describing to an agent.

generative-face.name: Generative Face
generative-face.short: CB used to run forward: typed claims compose into contracts, and contracts compile to artifacts - runnable tests and natural-language instruction files like CLAUDE.md and skills.
generative-face.def: The generative face is CB used to send knowledge out: typed claims compose into contracts (the contract interpreters are core), and contracts compile to artifacts - runnable tests and natural-language instruction files like CLAUDE.md and skills. The key instantiation is that a pre-registered eval rubric is a contract that compiles to both the runnable checks and the writeup. It is one of the three faces, the forward-running half, and it is a capability exercised when a real use pulls it.

graph-index-of-obligation.name: Graph as the Index of Obligation
graph-index-of-obligation.short: Work-to-do lives in the graph as grounded, subject-scoped, conflict-audited, staleness-linked directives; plans and docs hold records, never live todos.
graph-index-of-obligation.def: The graph is the index of obligation (cb:a489): work-to-do lives as directives - grounded, subject-scoped, conflict-audited, staleness-linked, and queryable - while plans/ and docs/ hold records, never live todos. The four-type schema gave work its own structural type, so the backlog can live where the reasoning lives. A live todo is an unmaterialized discrete directive, and the open question of whether sessions actually reach for the backlog over plan files is cb:a493. See obligation in the graph.

guidance.name: Guidance
guidance.short: The prescription (a directive) resting on a verdict; violated or withdrawn, never falsified, and kept a separate node from the finding by design.
guidance.def: Guidance is the eval directive: the prescription resting on a verdict, such as a ban or a routing rule, which is violated or withdrawn but never falsified. It is deliberately a separate node one step up from the finding, so a newer snapshot can falsify the verdict without ambiguity about what happens to the rule - the world falsifies the inference, and the house then decides what to do about its directives. In the worked example, sdl:a009 deps on the verdict sdl:a008.

harness.name: Harness
harness.short: The external runner that executes a model over cases (Inspect), living in the bench repo, never in CB; CB is the ledger, not the lab bench.
harness.def: The harness is the external runner that executes a model over cases (Inspect, the UK AISI framework), R times per condition, writing native logs (bench/). CB never executes anything: the boundary is CB is the ledger, not the lab bench (docs/eval-ledger.md). The harness touches CB through exactly one contract, the run-manifest emitted by a thin adapter, which is why CB never grows toward execution.

identity-hashing.name: Identity Hashing
identity-hashing.short: Eval belief ids are hashed from the identity tuple, never content, so re-import is a detected no-op and a changed run under the same id is a hard error; a corrected run is a new run_id.
identity-hashing.def: Eval belief ids are derived by hashing the identity tuple, never the content: <ns>:o-<hash> where the hash is the first 8 hex of a sha256 over a prefixed join of eval_id, run_id, ruler, and optional case_id (Manifest.hash). Dates come from the manifest, never the clock, so the same manifest yields byte-identical beliefs and a re-import is a detected no-op. A changed run under an existing observation id is a hard error, because observations are immutable - a corrected run is a new run_id. This is immutability enforced at the import boundary.

immutability.name: Immutability
immutability.short: Beliefs are never edited in place; the only changes are status transitions and appending evidence, so the history the DAG exists to preserve is never erased.
immutability.def: Beliefs are never edited in place (cb:a302): a change is a supersession (a new node plus a superseded_by link) or a retraction (a status flip with date and reason), and the lone sanctioned in-place edit is appending an evidence entry. Editing in place would erase the history the DAG exists to preserve; immutability forces supersession chains, which make state drift visible as stale deps rather than something a human must remember to check. The lifecycle is itself a state-machine contract, cb:c053. Two fields stay deliberately mutable because action and linkage history are orthogonal to truth status: materialized and the status-transition linkage. See immutability and supersession.

implies.name: implies
implies.short: A contract kind whose rules are {when, requires} routing rows naming a predicate to fire when a condition holds; the routing layer for codepath assertions and method checks.
implies.def: implies is a catalogued contract kind (cb:c046): its rules are {when, requires} rows yielding implies(When, Requires), read by CB.Belief.Contract.Implies (implies.ex), which reports which predicate slugs fire for a given field map. It is the routing layer for both codepath assertions (routing on assertions: on) and methodology checks (routing on verify: collection). The named predicate is implemented in Elixir and resolved through the predicate gate, never stored as code. The contract rule-kind implies sits on the prescriptive side, which is why the descriptive type was renamed away from implication.

import.name: Import
import.short: The write step that actually applies new beliefs to the graph (mix cb.import / cb.import.eval), via the atomic store write; dry-run by default.
import.def: Import is the write step that applies new beliefs to the graph: mix cb.import for a batch spec and mix cb.import.eval for a run-manifest, both dry-run by default and writing only with --write (cb.import.ex). It bottoms out in the single store write path with its atomic tmp-then-rename. The eval importer (import.eval) emits observation primitives only, leaving compounds and verdicts to the human write flow.

import-eval.name: import.eval
import-eval.short: mix cb.import.eval: validates a run-manifest, materializes observation primitives only, preflights each, and on --write hands them to the normal import apply path.
import-eval.def: mix cb.import.eval <manifest.json> --collection <path> (Mix.Tasks.Cb.Import.Eval) validates a run-manifest, materializes beliefs via CB.Eval.Manifest.to_beliefs/2, preflights each against the collection, and on --write hands them to the normal import apply path (dry-run by default). It emits observation primitives only - cross-ruler compounds and verdicts are authored by a human - so the tool's shape enforces the division of labor. Its identity hashing makes re-import idempotent.

in-memory-model.name: In-Memory Model
in-memory-model.short: There is no graph object: in memory the graph is a flat list of belief structs whose edges are deps string pointers, indexed and traversed on demand by CB.Belief.Graph.
in-memory-model.def: There is no graph object in memory; the graph is a flat list of %CB.Belief{} structs whose edges are implicit deps string pointers (graph.ex). Traversal builds an index (%{id => belief}) and walks deps, superseded_by, and subjects by string lookup, computing reverse edges by scanning. It is a DAG by discipline, not by enforcement, which is why every recursive walker carries a visited-set cycle guard. The pure traversal engine CB.Belief.Graph always produces the same output for the same input.

inference.name: Inference
inference.short: Infer: a conclusion licensed to exceed what its deps jointly state, paying for that licence by being independently falsifiable.
inference.def: An inference records the operation infer: an ampliative conclusion licensed to exceed what its deps jointly state, generalizing or abducing beyond its premises (cb:c051, cb:a473, cb:a472). It pays for that licence by being independently falsifiable: an inference is built to be superseded by new evidence while its deps stay active, so a clean run on a newer snapshot kills the generalization without touching the observations it rested on. Scope-widening past the deps' subjects is the structural signature of inference, and the verifier interrogates the deps-to-claim relation rather than the author's mental act. In eval work a verdict is the inference face. See inference and licensing.

inference-contract-prototype.name: Inferences as Contract Prototypes
inference-contract-prototype.short: A behavioral invariant lives as a prompt-enforced directive until code crystallizes around it, at which point it is elevated to a code-enforced contract.
inference-contract-prototype.def: Inferences and plain directives are prototypes for contracts (cb:a173, cb:a174): a behavioral invariant lives as a directive, prompt-enforced, until code crystallizes around it, at which point it is elevated to a contract that is code-enforced via the verify_against_contract pattern. This is the maturation path from a claim the house merely states to one it mechanically holds, and it is why beliefs and contracts share one store.

inference-naming.name: Why 'Inference' over 'Implication'
inference-naming.short: The v2 rename of the derived type, because a description licensed to exceed its premises cannot honestly be called an implication.
inference-naming.def: The v1 schema called the ampliative derived type implication; v2 renamed it to inference (decision D1, cb:a473). The argument: an implication licensed to state more than its premises imply is a contradiction in terms, and the contract rule-kind implies sits on the prescriptive side, so reusing the word descriptively would put one lexeme on both sides of the is/ought boundary. Mark confirmed the rename for launch, explicitly accepting the immutable-history cost in favour of long-term terminology (cb:a470). The deleted implication field is one of the expunged fields.

inspection-only-predicates.name: Inspection-Only Predicates
inspection-only-predicates.short: The discipline (cb:c050) that routed predicates observe and never mutate: names end in ? or _check and resolve only to exported zero-arity booleans.
inspection-only-predicates.def: Inspection-only is the discipline that routed predicates observe and never mutate (cb:c050): a predicate name must end in ? or _check and resolve to an exported zero-arity boolean, and the resolver refuses anything else (predicate_gate.ex:21). This is what makes it safe for a contract in the graph to name a check that runs against live code. The dynamic verifier is the one place these predicates actually execute.

invariants-field.name: Invariants
invariants-field.short: A contract's list of always-true statements (the ALWAYS to the rules' Given/When/Then); non-empty rules or invariants is what makes a directive a contract.
invariants-field.def: invariants is a contract's list of statements that must always hold - the ALWAYS clause complementing the Given/When/Then of rules (cb:a300). A directive is contract-grade exactly when its rules or invariants are non-empty, the biconditional the verifier enforces (cb:c056, verifier.ex:187). Together rules and invariants make a contract the intermediate level of formality between prose and code.

keys-field.name: _keys Shadow Field
keys-field.short: A non-serialized MapSet recording which JSON keys a belief actually carried on disk, so to_map/1 round-trips byte-stably without churning untouched records.
keys-field.def: _keys is a non-serialized bookkeeping field: a MapSet of the JSON keys the source object actually had (belief.ex:73). It lets to_map/1 round-trip byte-stably - a field absent on disk stays absent, a field assigned in memory gets emitted - which is what lets the store rewrite the whole array on every write without churning untouched records (belief.ex:208). It is the mechanism that makes the single-file graph practical to version in git. The three expunged keys are stripped from _keys on load.

kind.name: Kind
kind.short: A belief's semantic classification, drawn from the closed 38-value enum cb:c039, which binds the allowed structural types via the kind-type table.
kind.def: kind is the belief's semantic classification, drawn from the closed 38-value enum registry cb:c039 (belief.ex:51, kinds such as observation, fact, policy, convention, schema, design-principle). The kind binds mood: through the kind-type table (cb:c057) each kind maps to its allowed structural types, converting the prescriptive/descriptive boundary from prose into a deterministic check. A kind absent from the table is unchecked, since the enum itself is the closure authority (cb:a399, cb:a397). See the belief, field by field.

kind-action-item.name: kind: action-item
kind-action-item.short: A prescriptive kind for a single completable task: a directive with contract:false and empty rules/invariants, distinct from a standing rule.
kind-action-item.def: kind: action-item marks a single completable task, distinct from a standing kind: rule (cb:a403). The verifier enforces its shape: an action-item must be a directive with contract: false and empty rules and invariants (verifier.ex:347). It is the natural lifecycle:discrete unit of the backlog, materialized into todos and then closed; a standing rule is lifecycle:recurring and never materializes.

kind-policy.name: kind: policy
kind-policy.short: A prescriptive kind for an intentional commitment, a domain truth true by convention, that is not mechanically enforced today and is mutually exclusive with contract:true.
kind-policy.def: kind: policy marks an intentional commitment or domain truth true by convention - something the house decides to hold - that is not mechanically enforced today (cb:a439, cb:a427). A policy is a directive with contract: false, mutually exclusive with contract-grade, and it typically carries a name. It contrasts with a contract, which is the same prescriptive mood elevated to machine-checkable grade once code crystallizes around it.

kind-type-table.name: Kind-Type Table
kind-type-table.short: The derivation table cb:c057 binding each kind to its allowed structural types: prescriptive kinds to directive only, descriptive kinds never to directive, dual kinds to all four.
kind-type-table.def: The kind-type table (cb:c057, a derivation-table contract, design cb:a472) binds each kind to the structural types it may take, turning the prescriptive/descriptive boundary into a deterministic check (kind binds mood). The 15 prescriptive kinds (policy, rule, action-item, convention, and so on) map to directive only; the 21 descriptive kinds (observation, fact, error-pattern, design-observation) map to primitive, compound, or inference; the two dual kinds map to all four (CB.Belief.Contract.Table, verifier.ex:156). A kind absent from the table is unchecked, since the kind enum is the closure authority. See the belief, field by field.

lap-log.name: Lap Log
lap-log.short: A scratch markdown file the agent appends to station-by-station so the operator can watch a working session live in a split.
lap-log.def: The lap log (cb:a497) is a scratch markdown file the agent appends to station-by-station (tmp/lap-log.md), so the operator can keep a working session observable live in a split. It is a deliberately ephemeral working surface, distinct from the durable chronicle and transcript, and it embodies the rule that session memory is a cache, not a store.

lending-library.name: Lending Library (lib:)
lending-library.short: The gentle on-ramp collection in belief-collections (namespace lib), a worked-example surface for learning the schema before building a real graph.
lending-library.def: The lending library (lib:, cb:a459) is the gentle on-ramp collection in belief-collections: a worked-example surface for learning the schema vocabulary before authoring a real graph. It is listed in collections.json alongside the framework and eval collections. It is one of several worked-example collections, the others including method:, sdl, and toy.

licensing.name: Licensing
licensing.short: The bargain at the heart of inference: a conclusion is permitted to exceed its deps precisely because it is made independently falsifiable.
licensing.def: Licensing is the bargain that defines inference: a conclusion is permitted to exceed what its deps jointly state, and it pays for that permission by being independently falsifiable (cb:a473, cb:a472, narrated in mental-model.md). The price of the licence is the falsification lifecycle - a generalization is built to be superseded by new evidence while its deps stay true. This is why the schema separates the ampliative type from the non-ampliative compound: the licence to generalize is granted only against the obligation to be killable.

lifecycle-tag.name: Lifecycle Tag
lifecycle-tag.short: The load-bearing tag distinguishing recurring directives (standing rules that never materialize) from discrete ones (completable work); the backlog query keys on discrete.
lifecycle-tag.def: The lifecycle: tag is load-bearing (cb:a491): a recurring directive is a standing rule that never materializes, while a discrete directive is completable work. The backlog query keys on it - mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete is the desk. An untagged directive degrades the backlog view, which is why the tag is treated as a convention authors must apply. See obligation in the graph.

materialization.name: Materialization
materialization.short: Turning a directive into concrete action items through a sink and recording the link back on the belief's materialized field; a recorded test run counts too.
materialization.def: Materialization turns a directive into concrete action items: it validates the spec, confirms the belief is a directive not already materialized, runs a sink, and writes the link back onto the belief's materialized field (CB.Belief.Materializer, materializer.ex:49). The /materialize skill produces concrete todos; a recorded test run counts as materialization too (mix cb.verify.codepath --record). It is how obligation in the graph becomes traceable execution, with the link carrying provenance back to the belief.

materialized-field.name: Materialized
materialized-field.short: The {plan, date, last_verified} record linking a directive to the action items it was executed into; a historical fact, mutable because action history is orthogonal to truth.
materialized-field.def: materialized records a historical fact: that a directive was executed into concrete action items at a given date, as {plan, date, last_verified} plus todo refs (cb:a383, materializer.ex). It does not assert that the effects still hold, since code drifts; last_verified distinguishes historical execution from current verification, which a drift audit re-checks. It is one of the two deliberately mutable fields, because action and linkage history are orthogonal to truth status. An active directive with no materialized items is an open obligation - a live todo.

materializer.name: Materializer / Sink
materializer.short: CB.Belief.Materializer turns a directive into action items and links them back; the Sink behaviour is the host-plug seam where those items land, default Sink.JSON appending todos.
materializer.def: CB.Belief.Materializer (materializer.ex) runs the pipeline directive to action items to sink to link, writing the result onto the belief's materialized field. The CB.Materializer.Sink behaviour is the host-plug seam - where action items actually land is the host's concern - with the default Sink.JSON appending generic todo records and Sink.Test treating a test run as materialization. This is the code behind obligation becoming traceable work.

method-check.name: Method Check
method-check.short: The static pass running an eval collection's routed methodology predicates (implies rules routing on verify: collection) as deterministic graph traversal during cb.verify.collection.
method-check.def: The method check (CB.Method.Checks, method/checks.ex) is the static pass that discovers active contract-grade implies contracts whose rules route on verify: collection and invokes each routed predicate over the union, as pure deterministic graph traversal (eval/predicates.ex). It runs the methodology contracts during mix cb.verify.collection, and a failure names the offending belief ids - the failure message is the work order. It is the static counterpart to the dynamic codepath verifier.

methodology-contract.name: Methodology Contract
methodology-contract.short: A method: contract whose implies rule routes to a named predicate run during cb.verify.collection; the six are m-corroboration, provenance, subjects, runs, judge-validation, correction.
methodology-contract.def: A methodology contract is a method: contract-grade belief whose implies rule routes to a named predicate in CB.Eval.Predicates, run as a static method check during mix cb.verify.collection (eval-ledger.md). The six are m-corroboration (method:c4), m-provenance (c5), m-subjects (c6), m-runs (c7), m-judge-validation (c8), and m-correction (c9), plus method:c10 binding a verdict to inference. Strip the eval vocabulary and what remains is general agent epistemics: seek independent confirmation, cite raw evidence, never generalize from one sample, calibrate your judges, revise visibly (paradigm:a364).

mutation.name: Mutation
mutation.short: A typed, atom-keyed in-memory edit (add-dep, supersede, append-evidence, and others) dispatched purely by CB.Belief.Mutation, with the I/O handled by callers.
mutation.def: A mutation is a typed, atom-keyed in-memory edit dispatched purely by CB.Belief.Mutation (mutation.ex): the types include add-dep, drop-dep, supersede, retract, new-belief, drop-field, and append-evidence. Each mutation (except context and new-belief) appends a dated session: evidence entry recording the change, and a batch short-circuits on the first error so callers can roll back by discarding the partial result. Mutations are the in-memory edit layer beneath the write flow; I/O stays with the caller.

name-field.name: Name / Who
name-field.short: Two optional struct fields: name is a human handle for a belief; who is an optional attribution of where the claim came from.
name-field.def: name is an optional human-readable handle for a belief (used so contracts can be referenced as dag-structural-types rather than by id), and who is an optional attribution string (belief.ex). Both are convenience metadata layered on the namespaced id; CB.Belief.Store.find/1 resolves a belief by id or by name. Neither carries provenance, which lives in the artifact and evidence fields.

namespace.name: Namespace
namespace.short: The prefix of a belief id naming its owning collection; exactly one owning collection per namespace, ids globally unique, and cb: depends only within cb:.
namespace.def: A namespace is the prefix of a belief id naming the collection it belongs to (cb:a463): ids are namespace:local-id, exactly one owning collection per namespace, globally unique. Deps may cross namespaces, but cb: depends only within cb: while every other collection may depend on cb:, which keeps the framework self-contained and shippable. Bare ids resolve when unambiguous.

namespaced-id.name: Namespaced ID
namespaced-id.short: A belief's identity, namespace:local-id (cb:c029); a bare local id resolves to its unique namespaced match across the loaded graph.
namespaced-id.def: Every belief id is a namespaced string, namespace:local-id such as cb:c029 or agent-behavior:a054, globally unique with exactly one owning collection per namespace (cb:a463). The local-id prefix encodes grade: c### for a contract, a### for everything else, o-<hash> for an eval observation, t#### for a todo. A bare id (c029) resolves to its unique namespaced match (CB.Belief.Graph.resolve_id, graph.ex:22), the shared id-resolution primitive nearly every mix task uses.

nl-above-substrate.name: Natural Language Above the Substrate
nl-above-substrate.short: Prose discussion is productive exactly when every claim terminates in an anchor into the running code, which is the only artifact whose meaning is enforced by execution.
nl-above-substrate.def: The principle behind answer-time anchoring: natural-language discussion above the code is productive exactly when every claim terminates in an anchor into the running substrate (cb:a485). Code is the only artifact whose meaning is enforced by execution, so it is the operational substrate rather than a persisting abstraction layer (cb:a484), and reading it re-scopes to the anchored stops that carry an argument, making the editor an adjudication surface where claims meet what runs (cb:a486, cb:a487).
nl-above-substrate.also: code as operational substrate, editor as adjudication surface

no-confidence-scores.name: No Confidence Scores
no-confidence-scores.short: CB has no confidence field by design, because subjective scalars synthesized without a deterministic basis do no load-bearing work; rank by evidence, not vibes.
no-confidence-scores.def: CB has no confidence field by design (cb:a448, restated cb:a481): a subjective scalar synthesized without a deterministic basis does no load-bearing work and hides what it pretends to measure. In its place, CB.Belief.support/1 returns deterministic structural support counts, so a thin single-source claim and a load-bearing well-evidenced one are distinguishable by structure - rank by evidence, not vibes (mental-model.md). The field was deprecated rather than deleted (cb:a390), leaving the path open to a derived confidence if a concrete use-case ever materializes. See why there are no confidence scores.

no-emdashes.name: No Em-Dashes / No Contrastive Shape
no-emdashes.short: House prose rules: use hyphens, never em-dashes or en-dashes; and state what things are directly rather than using the X-does-not-Y-it-Z contrastive shape.
