no-emdashes.def: Two house prose rules govern every surface in this tree, including this glossary: use hyphens only, never em-dashes or en-dashes (cb:a455), and state what things are directly rather than reaching for the contrastive X-does-not-Y-it-Z shape (cb:a505). These are compiled formatting conventions, enforced because consistent prose is part of what makes the graph's output trustworthy. They are small instances of the broader principle that conventions in the graph are checkable.

no-maintenance-hatch.name: No Maintenance Hatch
no-maintenance-hatch.short: Framework canon changes only through its own write flow, even at the foundations, so audit-trail completeness is structural, not behavioral.
no-maintenance-hatch.def: There is no maintenance hatch (cb:a498): the framework's canon changes only through its own write flow, even at the foundations - the status-lifecycle contract was superseded via the lifecycle it defines (c029 to c053), and the type enum was replaced through adjudication by a node typed in the values it introduces (c026 to c051). Because no door bypasses the write flow, audit-trail completeness is structural, not behavioral: the history can be trusted without trusting that everyone behaved. This is the deepest justification for immutability and the reason axiom is a role, never a privileged type.

non-derivable-knowledge.name: Non-Derivable Knowledge Only
non-derivable-knowledge.short: The DAG holds only knowledge that cannot be derived from elsewhere; material documented in code or docs is not duplicated into beliefs.
non-derivable-knowledge.def: The DAG holds only non-derivable knowledge (cb:a427): material already documented in code or docs is not duplicated into beliefs, and deductive consequences are computed on demand rather than stored (cb:a473). This keeps the graph a store of claims that could not be reconstructed otherwise, consistent with composition over retrieval. It is why the framework's own graph contains the schema's what and how but routes the why to other collections.

observation.name: Observation
observation.short: A kind:observation primitive recording one ruler's immutable measurement of a run; emitted mechanically by the importer, never superseded by a new snapshot.
observation.def: An observation is a kind: observation primitive recording one ruler's immutable measurement of a run, emitted mechanically by import.eval (eval/manifest.ex). The importer emits one aggregate observation per (run, ruler) pair always, plus a per-case observation only for cases a human marks load_bearing, so the judgment stays upstream while the importer stays mechanical. Observations are never superseded by a new snapshot - the model really did behave that way that day - which is why the staleness pivot lands on the verdict instead. Each carries the six-subject convention and an eval: URI.

okf.name: OKF (Open Knowledge Format)
okf.short: A portable agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology: plain markdown plus YAML frontmatter in a directory hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.
okf.def: OKF, the Open Knowledge Format, is a portable knowledge methodology: plain markdown plus YAML frontmatter organized in a directory hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index. The full standard layers OKF (the floor), Karpathy's LLM-wiki synthesis discipline, and Composable Beliefs (an opt-in ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true), defined by a conformance corpus and implemented once in Elixir (mix okf.*, lib/cb/okf/*). CB emits down to an OKF bundle and ingests up from one, both lossy. See OKF and the memory boundary.

okf-bridge.name: OKF Bridge Modules
okf-bridge.short: The lib/cb/okf/* modules projecting CB down to an OKF bundle and ingesting OKF up into CB primitives, plus a faithful Elixir port of the OKF conformance validator.
okf-bridge.def: The OKF bridge is the set of lib/cb/okf/* modules: CB.Okf.Emit projects the CB graph down to an OKF bundle (one tier: cb doc per belief, lossy), CB.Okf.Ingest ingests a bundle up into CB primitives (typed composition left to /assert), and CB.Okf.Validate is a faithful Elixir port of the Python reference validator whose findings carry stable codes for the cross-impl conformance suite. CB.Okf.Manifest and CB.Okf.Frontmatter round out byte-compatible bundle handling.

cb-okf.name: cb-okf: Collection
cb-okf.short: The OKF integration extension folded into composable-beliefs/okf/; the cb-okf namespace depends on cb and borrows its schema, adding an agent-index manifest and a tier model.
cb-okf.def: cb-okf: is CB's OKF integration extension, folded into composable-beliefs/okf/ (cb:a546), a namespace that depends on cb: and borrows its schema vocabulary. The name marks it as CB's dialect of OKF: the OKF floor (markdown + frontmatter + index) plus CB's additions - an agent-index manifest and a two-tier model - rather than being OKF-native. Its eval relevance (cb:a544) is to run the DAG-versus-prose study with an OKF/Karpathy agent-maintained wiki as the credible prose baseline the structured arm must beat.

output-target.name: Output Target
output-target.short: A kind:output-target directive whose rules declare how to compile beliefs into a file - a CLAUDE.md, a rule file, or a codepath - so every output line traces to one claim.
output-target.def: An output target is a kind: output-target directive whose rules declare how to compile beliefs into a file (CB.OutputTarget, output_target.ex). With render_sections it dereferences each listed belief id to its claim and emits markdown - the mechanism behind compiled documents like CLAUDE.md - so every output line traces to one belief claim. With render_steps and an entry it is a codepath render-spec. The contract enforces that the target's deps equal the union of the referenced belief ids.

persistence-across-sessions.name: Persistence Across Sessions
persistence-across-sessions.short: A claim written by one session or subagent is readable by every later one without re-derivation; the durable cross-boundary reasoning the framework exists to provide.
persistence-across-sessions.def: Persistence across sessions and subagents (cb:a339, cb:a340, cb:a341, cb:a110) is the core promise: a claim written by one session is readable by every later session and subagent without re-derivation. This is what makes the graph a shared prosthetic for attention and context, and it is why obligations and records must live in the graph and repos rather than in session memory.

plan.name: Plan
plan.short: What a large directive materializes into: a record of execution and history, retained permanently as a cited source doc, never the live source of obligation.
plan.def: A plan is what a large directive materializes into (cb:a490): the materialized field's plan path links the obligation to its execution record, and plans/ are retained permanently as cited source docs. Plans encode intent as history; static implementation steps in a plan are liabilities, because intent lives in the DAG while the world drifts (cb:a382, cb:a375). The reference direction inverts: query the graph for what is next, then follow it into plans/ for how and history.

position.name: Position
position.short: A document capturing a settled stance whose verbatim wording matters, with its typed claims then extracted into the graph; the epistemic artifact behind a codepath.
position.def: A position document captures a settled stance whose verbatim wording matters, and its claims are then extracted into the graph through the /position skill (cb:a492, cb:a528). It names the epistemic artifact (the stance plus its claims), while a codepath names the render face resolved from the position's anchors - a position with anchors has a codepath. Framework-policy positions live in the framework repo's positions/, because a distributable framework must resolve every artifact its own graph cites (cb:a492). See positions and chronicles.

position-vs-codepath.name: Position vs Codepath
position-vs-codepath.short: Two names for two faces of one thing: position names the epistemic artifact (stance plus claims), codepath names the render face (the navigable tour from its anchors).
position-vs-codepath.def: Position and codepath name two faces of one thing (cb:a528): a position names the epistemic artifact - the settled stance and its typed claims - while a codepath names the render face, the navigable tour resolved from the position's anchors. The slogan is that a position with anchors has a codepath. This mirrors the broader compiled-versus-rendered split between what faces the agent and what faces the human.

predicate-gate.name: Predicate Gate
predicate-gate.short: The shared resolve gate (CB.PredicateGate) between a predicate name stored in the DAG and executable code: it enforces the inspection-only name and the expected arity.
predicate-gate.def: The predicate gate (CB.PredicateGate, predicate_gate.ex:31) is the shared resolve point between a predicate name stored in the graph and the executable code it refers to. It enforces two checks per cb:c050: the name matches the inspection-only pattern, and it resolves to an exported function of the expected arity. Anything else is refused, because an executable string in the graph has nothing to grab onto - the routing/implementation boundary at the predicate level. The two predicate worlds (codepath arity-0 and collection arity-2) share only this gate.

preflight.name: Preflight
preflight.short: The read-only authoring-time check (mix cb.preflight) that classifies a proposed belief's overlaps with the live graph into conflicting, schema, supportive, and neutral buckets.
preflight.def: Preflight (CB.Belief.Conflict, conflict.ex) is the read-only authoring-time check that compares a proposed belief against the live graph and buckets matches: contract-level conflicts (which block the write, exit 2), schema conflicts, supportive matches (dep candidates), and neutral matches. It matches on three axes - subject overlap, tag overlap, and claim token overlap above a threshold - and classifies a match as conflicting only with semantic contact (cb:c064). It is the mechanism that surfaces conflicts at authoring time, the heart of the consensus mechanism.

preflight-escalation.name: Preflight Escalation Rule
preflight-escalation.short: The contract cb:c064 that a match escalates to a conflict bucket only with semantic contact, not bare tag overlap, enforcing at preflight what conflict scope states as doctrine.
preflight-escalation.def: The preflight escalation rule (cb:c064, cb:a519) governs when a match escalates: only with semantic contact (a shared subject ref or claim overlap) does a match move into a conflict bucket, never on bare tag overlap alone. It is the contract that makes preflight enforce, at write time, the doctrine that conflict scope states - that overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction. Contract-grade matches always carry a contract-level priority regardless.

primitive.name: Primitive
primitive.short: Attest: one atomic statement of what a single source said, grounded in an artifact plus evidence, carrying no deps.
primitive.def: A primitive records the operation attest: one atomic statement of what a single source said, grounded in an artifact and dated evidence, with no deps (cb:c051, cb:a303). Atomicity here is about provenance, not logic (cb:a475, cb:a298): what makes a claim primitive is that it derives from one source rather than from other beliefs, so a primitive conjoining separable assertions is a mis-authored compound that should be split at authoring time. A primitive is an immutable record, only superseded if the source itself is re-read differently. In eval work, an observation is the primitive face.

primitive-atomicity.name: Primitive Atomicity
primitive-atomicity.short: The doctrine that what makes a claim primitive is deriving from one source, not being logically simple; a conjoined primitive is a mis-authored compound.
primitive-atomicity.def: Primitive atomicity (cb:a475, cb:a298) is the doctrine that atomicity is about provenance, not logic: a claim is a primitive because it derives from one source rather than from other beliefs. A primitive that conjoins separable assertions is therefore a mis-authored compound and should be split at authoring time, with the conjunction expressed as a compound depending on the split primitives. This keeps the single-source attestation honest and lets the graph distinguish what one source said from what several sources jointly support.

problem-of-induction.name: Problem of Induction
problem-of-induction.short: The unbridgeable gap from finitely many observations to a universal claim, which is exactly the gap an inference is licensed to cross.
problem-of-induction.def: The problem of induction is that no finite set of observations deductively entails a universal generalization about all cases. CB makes this gap a structural feature: the premises of an inference are about particular cases (case 7 of run 3), while its conclusion is about every such case ever, and no chain of deductive steps bridges that distance (cb:a473). The schema does not pretend to close the gap; it licenses the leap and then makes the resulting claim falsifiable, so a single counter-run can retire the generalization.

proving-ground.name: Proving Ground
proving-ground.short: Scoping the public wedge to evals does not shelve CB's grand capability set; the wedge exercises it - single-agent self-knowledge is continuous with the group-scale failures evals study.
proving-ground.def: The proving-ground claim resolves the worry that scoping the public wedge to evals abandons CB's broader ambition: narrowing to evals does not shelve the grand capability set, the wedge exercises it. CB's single-agent self-knowledge capability is the individual-scale form of the group-scale failures the eval vertical studies (conformity collapse, monoculture), and the two are continuous - the same epistemic failure at two scales. The eval vertical is therefore the proving domain for the full vision, not a retreat from it.

pure-graph-over-one-file.name: Pure Graph Over One File
pure-graph-over-one-file.short: The whole framework is deterministic graph code over one JSON file: no database, no server, no LLM call in lib/, so the same modules drive mix tasks and a long-lived LiveView.
pure-graph-over-one-file.def: The framework is pure deterministic graph code over one JSON file (composable-beliefs/lib): there is no database, no server, and no LLM call anywhere in lib/. Every public function returns {:ok, _} or {:error, _} and nothing raises across module boundaries except a deliberate halt in mix tasks, so the same modules drive both mix tasks and a long-lived LiveView. This determinism is what gives CB its LLM-free read path. See the architecture.

reasoning-substrate-not-memory.name: Reasoning Substrate, Not Memory
reasoning-substrate-not-memory.short: CB is a reasoning and audit substrate that layers over any memory or recall system; building retrieval in would forfeit the deterministic, LLM-free read path the framework exists to provide.
reasoning-substrate-not-memory.def: CB is a reasoning and audit substrate, not a memory system (cb:a539): it layers over any memory or recall system rather than becoming one, and building retrieval into CB would forfeit the deterministic, LLM-free read path the framework exists to provide. OKF is the prose and knowledge layer; CB is the verifiable-claims layer. This is the boundary that keeps the framework's value in composition rather than recall. See OKF and the memory boundary.

recency.name: Recency
recency.short: A pure event view of what changed in a window - new nodes, supersessions, evidence appends, materializations, retractions - so concurrent activity is observable.
recency.def: The recency view (cb:a501, shipped as mix bs recent, CB.Belief.Graph.recent, graph.ex:272) reports what changed in a window: new nodes, supersessions, evidence appends (strictly after creation), materializations, and retractions. The window test is a lexical ISO-date compare on the created and entry dates, so no git history is needed. It exists so that concurrent multi-session activity is observable without reading the commit log, supporting the discipline to author against the live graph.

receptive-face.name: Receptive Face
receptive-face.short: CB used to record, supersede, and audit findings - typed primitives, provenance, an audit tree from a verdict down to the raw run; this is the ledger, the receptive half of the substrate.
receptive-face.def: The receptive face is CB used to take knowledge in: record findings as typed primitives, supersede them as evidence changes, and audit a verdict down to the raw run via the audit tree. This is exactly the eval ledger the tutorial teaches, and it is genuinely valuable as the audit and tamper-evidence reading. It is one of the three faces, the receptive half of the substrate.

retired.name: Retired
retired.short: The directive-only lifecycle exit: a withdrawn rule no longer in force; descriptive claims are superseded or retracted, never retired.
retired.def: Retired is the directive-only terminal status: a rule the house has stopped standing behind and that is no longer in force (cb:c053, verifier.ex:500). Descriptive claims (primitives, compounds, inferences) are superseded or retracted rather than retired, because they were never in force to begin with (cb:a471). The verifier enforces that only directives carry this status, which is one of the structural ways the is/ought boundary shows up in the lifecycle.

retraction.name: Retraction
retraction.short: Withdrawing a belief as wrong, recording retracted_on and retracted_reason; distinct from supersession, which replaces with a successor.
retraction.def: Retraction withdraws a belief as wrong or moot, flipping its status to retracted and recording retracted_on and retracted_reason (cb:c053, cb.retract.ex, mutation.ex:137). It differs from supersession, which names a successor; a retracted node simply should not stand. A belief that deps on a retracted node becomes detectably stale. Eval corrections are dated supersessions rather than bare retractions, enforced by contract method:c9.

retro-pairing.name: Retro-Pairing
retro-pairing.short: Pairing a session: stipulation with a document: pointer so a fresh agent can reach the grounding of a minted belief cold.
retro-pairing.def: Retro-pairing (cb:a507, cb:a540) pairs a session: stipulation with a document: pointer, so a belief grounded in an ephemeral session can still be reached cold by a later agent. It is the concrete practice that makes a directive self-bootstrapping, and it is one half of why session artifacts need to dereference.

routed-predicate.name: Routed Predicate
routed-predicate.short: A predicate referenced by name in a contract's implies rules but implemented in Elixir, resolved through the gate; the graph routes, code implements.
routed-predicate.def: A routed predicate is named in a contract's implies rules but implemented in Elixir, resolved through the predicate gate (codepath/predicates.ex, eval/predicates.ex). It is the running embodiment of the routing/implementation boundary (cb:c047): the graph expresses which predicate fires on which condition, and the module carries the body. Codepath predicates are zero-arity and app-reading; methodology predicates are arity-2 and graph-reading.

routing-implementation-boundary.name: Routing / Implementation Boundary
routing-implementation-boundary.short: The keystone rule (cb:c047): contracts carry routing tables saying which predicate fires when; modules carry the predicate implementations; the graph never stores executable code.
routing-implementation-boundary.def: The routing/implementation boundary (cb:c047) is the keystone: contracts carry routing tables expressing which predicates fire on which conditions, while modules carry the predicate implementations, and the graph never stores executable code. This is what makes it safe for the graph to drive tests, since an executable string in the DAG would have nothing to grab onto (predicate_gate.ex). The same boundary appears in the state-machine that routes status edges without evaluating their requirements, and in every routed predicate. See contracts.

rule-kind-catalogue.name: Rule-Kind Catalogue
rule-kind-catalogue.short: The closed registry of contract rule kinds (cb:c046) - state-machine, enum-registry, derivation-table, implies, output-target - each a Datalog fact shape plus one Elixir interpreter.
rule-kind-catalogue.def: Contract rules decompose into a closed catalogue of rule kinds (cb:c046): each kind has a Datalog-shaped declarative fact and exactly one Elixir interpreter. The five are state-machine (edge/3), enum-registry (allowed/2), derivation-table (row/N), implies (implies/2), and output-target (field/2). Datalog supplies the fact shape only; evaluation lives in each kind's ordinary interpreter - the graph routes, code implements (CB.Belief.Contract.*). The catalogue is itself a contract, an instance of the graph describing itself.

ruler.name: Ruler
ruler.short: CB's word for a scorer or judge - a deterministic differ or an LLM judge alike - that turns one model output into an outcome; a subject of type ruler.
ruler.def: A ruler is CB's word for a scorer or judge, a deterministic field-differ or an LLM judge alike, that turns one model output into an outcome (eval-ledger.md). It appears as a subject of type ruler on every observation, and an LLM-judge ruler requires a human-agreement validation record (contract method:c8). Two independent rulers reaching the same outcome is the basis of a cross-ruler agreement compound.

rules-field.name: Rules
rules-field.short: A contract's list of routing/relation rows - a tiny Datalog relation - read by exactly one interpreter module per kind; empty on non-contracts.
rules-field.def: rules is a contract's list of routing or relation rows, a tiny Datalog relation whose shape depends on the contract's kind and is read by exactly one interpreter module (CB.Belief.Contract.*). A directive is contract-grade iff its rules or invariants are non-empty (the contract biconditional, cb:c056). The rules express which predicate fires on which condition; they never store executable code, the routing/implementation boundary (cb:c047). See the rule-kind catalogue.

run-manifest.name: Run-Manifest
run-manifest.short: The neutral version-1 JSON record of one harness execution; the single contract between bench and ledger, and the only door into the graph.
run-manifest.def: The run-manifest (docs/run-manifest.md, CB.Eval.Manifest) is the neutral version-1 JSON record of one harness execution: eval_id, date, model, model_version, harness identity, and a list of runs whose scorers (rulers) carry outcome counts and optional load-bearing cases. It is the single contract between the harness and the ledger - CB never learns any harness's native log format - and the only door into the graph. A thin per-harness adapter writes it; import.eval reads it.

schema-as-contracts.name: Schema as Contracts
schema-as-contracts.short: The schema is expressed as contracts inside the graph; mix cb.verify.schema checks the CB.Belief struct and the live graph against them, so code and declared schema cannot silently drift.
schema-as-contracts.def: The schema describes itself: its rules are expressed as contracts inside the graph (cb:c051 through cb:c059, cb:c066), and mix cb.verify.schema checks both the CB.Belief struct and the live graph against them (cb:a482). The graph is therefore simultaneously the example and the specification, and the framework's own graph is verified by the same code path as every borrower's. This is the dogfooding that makes the no maintenance hatch principle real at the level of the schema. See the graph describes itself.

schema-discipline.name: Schema Discipline
schema-discipline.short: The umbrella contract cb:c056 binding provenance via artifact, the contract biconditional, the ban on the implication field, enum binding, and kind-type binding.
schema-discipline.def: Schema discipline (cb:c056, supersedes cb:c038) is the umbrella contract binding several rules into one: provenance flows through the artifact field; contract: true is biconditional with non-empty rules or invariants; no belief carries the deleted implication field; enum-shaped fields bind to their enum contracts; and kind binds structural type via the kind-type table (cb:a481). It is one of the contracts the schema uses to describe and check itself.

schema-verifier.name: Schema Verifier
schema-verifier.short: CB.Schema.Verifier: the pure engine that checks a collection against the contracts it carries, discovering enums and lifecycle by role with no hardcoded ids.
schema-verifier.def: CB.Schema.Verifier (verifier.ex) is the pure engine behind verify.schema: it runs around twenty checks, splitting framework-universal structure (checked against the CB.Belief canon) from collection-specific vocabulary discovered by role. The load-bearing checks include the type enum, the contract biconditional, the discovered enums, the kind-type table, subject containment, and grounding. It is the dogfooding engine that lets the schema check itself.

self-bootstrapping.name: Self-Bootstrapping Directive
self-bootstrapping.short: A backlog directive reachable cold through its deps and resolvable document/plan artifacts, so a fresh session can pick it up without a handoff.
self-bootstrapping.def: A self-bootstrapping directive (cb:a507) is a backlog node reachable cold: a fresh session can reconstruct what to do from its deps and its resolvable document: or plan: artifacts, with no handoff prose. This is what makes the desk usable across sessions, and it is enforced in practice by retro-pairing every minted belief with a document pointer.

self-referential-beliefs.name: Self-Referential Beliefs
self-referential-beliefs.short: Beliefs whose subject is the agent itself - its patterns and failure modes - written with the normal schema and a subject of type agent.
self-referential-beliefs.def: A self-referential belief takes the agent itself as its subject - its own patterns, errors, and failure modes - using the ordinary schema with a subject of type agent (cb:a115, thesis). This is the schema-level capability that the capstone's self-referential face puts to work: a system gaining a queryable model of its own failure modes that it can consult before acting. The failure-mode collection agent-behavior: is built largely from such beliefs.

self-referential-face.name: Self-Referential Face
self-referential-face.short: CB used to give a system a queryable model of its own failure modes - claims whose subject is an agent itself, that it can consult before acting so it resists the next failure.
self-referential-face.def: The self-referential face is CB used to take an agent itself as subject: claims about a system's own failure modes, giving it a queryable model of how it tends to fail that it can consult before acting (docs/actualization.md). It is the schema capability of self-referential beliefs put to work as the third of the three faces. It is the single-agent form of the group-scale failures the eval vertical studies, the actualize rung of the ladder.

semantic-contact.name: Semantic Contact
semantic-contact.short: The escalation trigger that promotes a preflight match to a conflict: a shared subject ref or claim overlap; bare tag overlap is family resemblance, not contact.
semantic-contact.def: Semantic contact is the escalation trigger in preflight: a match becomes a conflict only when it has a shared subject ref or claim overlap, because bare tag overlap is family resemblance rather than contact (conflict.ex:230, cb:c064). It enforces at write time what conflict scope states as doctrine - overlap is necessary but not sufficient for contradiction. This is what keeps the conflict bucket from filling with merely tag-adjacent neighbours.

session-artifacts-resolvable.name: Session Artifacts Resolvable (Future)
session-artifacts-resolvable.short: An open gap: session: slugs dereference to nothing today; the proposed fix is a registry mapping each slug to the uuid-keyed jsonl transcript on disk.
session-artifacts-resolvable.def: Session artifacts must become resolvable (cb:a518): today a session: slug dereferences to nothing, so the proposed fix is a registry mapping each slug to the uuid-keyed jsonl transcript on disk. Until it lands, retro-pairing a session stipulation with a document: pointer is the workaround that keeps a grounded belief reachable cold.

session-memory-cache.name: Session Memory as Ephemeral Cache
session-memory-cache.short: Project and work state are banned from agent memory; the graph owns obligations, repos own records, and the bootstrap compiles from the graph - the digest doctrine applied to the agent's own notebook.
session-memory-cache.def: Session memory is an ephemeral cache, never a store (cb:a509): project and work state are banned from it, because the graph owns obligations, repos own records, and the bootstrap compiles from the graph. It is the digest-file antipattern doctrine applied to the agent's own notebook - a remembered snapshot rots the moment another concurrent session pushes, which is why the discipline is to author against the live graph. Load-bearing items are promoted through the write flow, then pruned.

shared-prosthetic.name: Shared Prosthetic
shared-prosthetic.short: The graph compensates for both human attention-bounds and agent context-loss with one queryable structure, serving person and agent at once.
shared-prosthetic.def: CB is a shared prosthetic (cb:a460): one queryable structure that compensates for both human attention-bounds and agent context-loss at the same time. The human cannot hold a large reasoning graph in working memory and the agent cannot carry it across context windows, so both lean on the same DAG. It is why the graph serves persistence across sessions and why obligation and records live in it rather than in any one participant's memory.

six-subject-convention.name: Six-Subject Convention
six-subject-convention.short: The subject set every observation must carry - eval, run, case, model, model_version, ruler - so every provenance dimension of a measurement is queryable.
six-subject-convention.def: The six-subject convention (contract method:c6) is the subject set every observation must carry: eval, run, case, model, model_version, and ruler (eval/predicates.ex). Because an eval result's natural provenance fields all land on the existing CB schema as subjects, tags, the artifact URI, and the evidence entry, every provenance dimension is queryable with zero new fields or query code. The model_version subject is also the staleness pivot.

skip-not-fail.name: Skip, Not Fail
skip-not-fail.short: A collection that declares no contract for a field has that check skipped rather than failed, so borrowers are checked only against the vocabulary they actually carry.
skip-not-fail.def: When a collection declares no governing contract for a field, the verifier skips that check rather than failing it (verifier.ex). A collection is therefore checked only against the vocabulary it actually carries or borrows, which is what makes discovery by role safe across collections of different completeness. The result vocabulary is the three-way :ok | :fail | :skip, and a skip is a neutral outcome.

sources-are-not-beliefs.name: Sources Are Not Beliefs
sources-are-not-beliefs.short: A source is raw material from which primitives are extracted; the graph stores structured beliefs, not the documents they came from.
sources-are-not-beliefs.def: A source is raw material, not a belief (cb:a113, cb:a114): the graph stores structured primitives extracted from a source, while the source itself lives as a referenced artifact. This is why a multi-paragraph analysis is a source document citing belief ids inline rather than a belief with a giant claim. The extraction happens at read time, never retroactively.

specify-deduce-actualize.name: Specify, Deduce, Actualize
specify-deduce-actualize.short: The three-step ladder beyond the ledger: specify the eval as a contract, deduce remediation from the belief network, then actualize by feeding findings back as self-referential claims a system queries.
specify-deduce-actualize.def: The ladder beyond the ledger has three rungs, each a capability pulled when a use demands it: specify the eval as a contract (a DSL that compiles to checks), deduce remediation from the belief network (the improvement flywheel), and actualize by feeding findings back as self-referential claims a system queries before acting, so it resists the next failure. Specify is near-term (Ring 1); deduce and actualize are the vision horizon. It is the generative face expressed as a sequence.

staleness.name: Staleness
staleness.short: An active non-primitive belief that deps on a superseded or retracted node; detectable by query, never something a human must remember to check.
staleness.def: Staleness is detectable, not remembered: an active non-primitive belief whose dep was superseded or retracted shows in mix bs stale (CB.Belief.Graph.stale, graph.ex:202), with --cascade propagating transitively. This is the payoff of immutability - state drift surfaces as a structural fact rather than a thing to audit by memory. In eval work, the staleness pivot on model_version is what lets a new snapshot flag the downstream directives for review. See staleness and recency.

staleness-pivot.name: Staleness Pivot
staleness-pivot.short: The convention (method:a2) that verdicts are scoped to a model_version, so a new snapshot supersedes the verdict (not the observations) and --cascade flags the dependent directives.
staleness-pivot.def: The staleness pivot (method:a2) is the convention that a verdict is scoped to a model_version subject, so when a re-run on a new snapshot changes the answer, the verdict is superseded while the immutable observations stay true. Then mix bs stale --cascade flags the downstream guidance directives for review. It is the move a flat spreadsheet cannot make honestly, and it is staleness detection applied to evals.

state-machine.name: state-machine
state-machine.short: A contract kind whose rules declare allowed status edges {from, to, requires}; the routing table behind the status lifecycle, which routes but does not evaluate requirements.
state-machine.def: state-machine is a catalogued contract kind (cb:c046): its rules declare {from, to, requires}, yielding edge(From, To, Requires) read by CB.Belief.Contract.StateMachine (state_machine.ex). It governs the status lifecycle (cb:c053) and the four-type contract cb:c051. It answers which edges exist and what each requires; it deliberately does not evaluate the requirement slugs, keeping the DSL tabular - the routing/implementation boundary in miniature.

status-lifecycle.name: Status Lifecycle
status-lifecycle.short: The state machine active -> superseded | retracted | retired; every non-active state is terminal, with required linkage, and retired is directive-only.
status-lifecycle.def: The status field is a closed enum governed by the state-machine contract cb:c053 (dag-status-lifecycle): every belief begins active and may transition to superseded (requires superseded_by), retracted (requires retracted_on and retracted_reason), or retired (directive-only). All non-active states are terminal and immutable except for appending evidence (belief.ex:49, CB.Belief.Contract.StateMachine). The contract was itself superseded through the very lifecycle it defines (c029 to c053), an instance of the no maintenance hatch principle.

status-vs-materialized.name: Status vs Materialized
status-vs-materialized.short: Two orthogonal axes: status asks whether the claim is still true; materialized asks whether the action was executed. Active and unmaterialized is still-actionable work.
status-vs-materialized.def: Status and materialized are orthogonal (cb:a380): status asks whether the claim is still true, while materialized asks whether the action was executed. The combinations are meaningful - active and unmaterialized is still-actionable work, while active and materialized is discharged at a time yet still true as a principle. Separating the two is what lets the backlog live in the graph: a live todo is an active directive not yet materialized.

stipulation-artifact.name: Stipulation Artifact
stipulation-artifact.short: The record of an adoption event that grounds a convention without manufacturing fake deps; the closed scheme subset plan, user, session, document.
stipulation-artifact.def: A stipulation artifact is the record of an adoption event - where a prescription was decided - letting a convention be grounded without manufacturing fake deps (cb:a476, cb:c059). The closed subset of schemes that can stipulate is plan, user, session, and document (the @stipulation_schemes, verifier.ex:373). It is how a user: or session: decision legitimately grounds a directive that derives from no other belief.

store-boundary.name: Store Boundary
store-boundary.short: CB.Belief.Store is the one sanctioned disk boundary for the graph: read/0 returns structs, write/2 maps every struct back through to_map and the atomic writer.
store-boundary.def: CB.Belief.Store (store.ex) is the only sanctioned disk boundary for the graph: read/0 returns a list of structs (a missing file yields an empty list), and write/2 maps every struct through to_map/1, encodes pretty JSON, and calls the atomic writer. The path comes from CB.Config.beliefs_path/0, so a --beliefs PATH flag just retargets it for one run, which is how alternate collections are queried without touching the main graph. One read path, one write path.

strict-aggregate.name: Strict-Aggregate Doctrine
strict-aggregate.short: The rule that a compound's claim states exactly the conjunction of its deps and nothing more; any interpretation or generalization is inference content, trimmed out.
strict-aggregate.def: The strict-aggregate doctrine (cb:a474) holds that a compound's claim states exactly the conjunction of what its deps say, with any interpretation or generalization trimmed out as inference content. The compound is the one truth-preserving entailment the schema stores (conjunction introduction, cb:a473); all other deductive consequences are infinite and free, so query tools compute them on demand rather than storing them. The compound's real work is selection and naming - asserting that two independent rulers agree is a claim neither dep makes alone yet is fully contained in what they say together.

structural-support.name: Structural Support
structural-support.short: The deterministic counts - artifacts, evidence entries, deps - returned by CB.Belief.support/1 in place of a subjective confidence number.
structural-support.def: Structural support is the deterministic substitute for a confidence score: CB.Belief.support/1 (belief.ex:150) returns %{artifact_count, evidence_count, dep_count}, shown on the Support: line of every record. Because the counts are read off structure, a thin claim and a load-bearing one are distinguishable by query rather than by a declared number (cb:a448). This is the concrete mechanism behind no confidence scores: trust comes from cited evidence and dependency structure, with no subjective scoring anywhere in the system.

structural-type.name: Structural Type
structural-type.short: The closed four-valued enum (primitive, compound, inference, directive), one per epistemic operation, that determines which other fields are meaningful.
structural-type.def: type is a closed enum of exactly four values, one per epistemic operation: primitive (attest), compound (aggregate), inference (infer), and directive (prescribe). The type a belief carries determines which other fields are meaningful and which checks apply, governed by the state-machine contract cb:c051 (design rationale cb:a470, cb:a298), which supersedes the older three-type cb:c026. The type string is authoritative in code; the verifier enforces per-type rules and the shell colours each type (lib/cb/belief.ex, CB.Schema.Verifier). See the four types.

subject-containment.name: Subject Containment
subject-containment.short: The invariant that an active compound's subject refs must be a subset of the union of its deps' subject refs; widening past it signals inference.
subject-containment.def: Subject containment (cb:c058, checked at CB.Schema.Verifier check_subject_containment, verifier.ex:448) is the formal shadow of the compound's non-ampliativity: an active compound's subject refs must be a subset of the union of its deps' subject refs, because a conjunction cannot be about anything its parts are not about. Scope-widening past that union is the structural signature of inference, so the same check that validates compounds also tells the two derived types apart. Empty subjects pass vacuously. See inference licensing.

subjects.name: Subjects
subjects.short: Belief-to-entity topical reference: {ref, type} pairs naming what a belief is about; drives subject containment and conflict scope.
subjects.def: subjects is the belief-to-entity relation of topical reference - what a belief is about - as {ref, type} pairs over files, modules, models, eval runs, and sometimes other beliefs (cb:a408, graph.ex:333). It is distinct from deps (logical derivation): a primitive like cb:a386 is about the digest file yet depends on nothing. Subjects drive subject containment for compounds and one axis of conflict scope for directives. Eval observations carry the six-subject convention.

supersession.name: Supersession
supersession.short: Replacing a belief with a better one: the old node flips to superseded with superseded_by pointing forward to its named successor.
supersession.def: Supersession is the immutable replacement of a belief: a new successor node is written and the old node flips to superseded with superseded_by pointing forward to it (cb:c053, cb:a302, adjudication.ex:160). The forward-linked chain is walked with mix bs history (CB.Belief.Graph.history, graph.ex:157), so every generation of a claim stays reachable. Supersession is how a re-run changes an eval verdict without rewriting the observations beneath it: the old verdict renders struck through with a link to its successor.

tags.name: Tags
tags.short: Flat cross-cutting labels on a belief; an axis of conflict overlap and the carrier of the load-bearing lifecycle and output-routing tags.
tags.def: tags are flat cross-cutting labels (belief.ex), used as one axis of conflict overlap and as the carrier of several load-bearing conventions: the lifecycle tag (lifecycle:discrete or lifecycle:recurring) that distinguishes completable work from standing rules, the output:codepath and output:claude-md tags that route output targets, and the fixture tag that quarantines synthetic data. Bare tag overlap alone is family resemblance, never semantic contact (cb:c064).

the-desk.name: The Desk
the-desk.short: The live obligation surface: the query mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete, returning unmaterialized discrete directives - the backlog a session pulls from.
the-desk.def: The desk is the live obligation surface: the query mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete, returning every unlinked directive tagged lifecycle:discrete (cb:a491, cb:a508). A session resumes by querying the desk rather than reading a handoff (cb:a508), and a desk directive must be self-bootstrapping. The cross-collection desk view (cb:a500) and a global directive graph in dotfiles (cb:a543) are open extensions.

thermometer-thermostat.name: Thermometer and Thermostat
thermometer-thermostat.short: A capability duality: the receptive face reads and measures (the thermometer), while the generative face diagnoses and generates control (the thermostat); one contract mechanism underlies both.
thermometer-thermostat.def: Thermometer and thermostat names the capability duality between the two outward faces: the receptive face reads and measures (the thermometer - record, measure, publish), while the generative face diagnoses and generates control (the thermostat - deduce remediation and compile guardrails). The shared spine is the contract: the same DSL contract compiles to a public eval's checks and to a private set of guardrails. The metaphor is a capability distinction (read versus generate); the business and monetization layer it can carry is held private.

three-faces.name: The Three Faces
three-faces.short: CB has three faces a consumer can put to work: receptive (record and audit), generative (compose contracts that compile to tests and instruction files), and self-referential (claims about an agent's own failure modes).
three-faces.def: The capstone framing is that CB has three faces a consumer can put to work: the receptive face (record, supersede, audit - the ledger), the generative face (typed claims compose into contracts that compile to tests and natural-language instruction files), and the self-referential face (claims whose subject is an agent's own failure modes). The substrate has faces, and the consumer is the one that puts those faces to work. Beyond the ledger means moving from using only the receptive face to using the generative and self-referential ones as well. See beyond the ledger.

three-tier-anchor-verification.name: Three-Tier Anchor Verification
three-tier-anchor-verification.short: The gradient of anchor checks - provenance integrity, grounding currency, behavioral validity - each a necessary condition only: failure flags for review, success alone never re-validates.
three-tier-anchor-verification.def: Anchor verification comes in three tiers (cb:a529): provenance integrity (source anchors - the quoted evidence still exists), grounding currency (code anchors - the referent still exists), and behavioral validity (predicates - it still behaves as claimed, contract grade). Each tier is a necessary condition only: a failed anchor flags a belief for review, while a passing anchor alone never re-validates the claim. This is the verification spine under staleness, answer-time anchoring, and the dynamic verifier.

tier-cb.name: tier: cb
tier-cb.short: The frontmatter marker on an OKF doc projected from a CB belief, carrying the belief id and cb_type so the bundle can round-trip back into the graph.
tier-cb.def: tier: cb is the frontmatter marker on an OKF doc projected from a CB belief, carrying the belief id and cb_type for round-tripping (okf/emit.ex:64, okf/validate.ex:139). The OKF type map projects each CB type to an OKF type (primitive to reference, compound to concept, inference to analysis, directive to position) while preserving the CB type in cb_type. A tier: cb doc requires an id, enforced by the validator.

todo-close.name: Todo-Close Front Door
todo-close.short: mix cb.todo.close: flips a materialized work item from open to done with discharge notes.
todo-close.def: The todo-close front door (mix cb.todo.close, cb:a530) flips a materialized work item from open to done with discharge notes (CB.Todos.close, todos.ex:61). It is one of the three sanctioned front doors. Note cb:a523: materialization notes currently get dropped by the JSON sink, an open gap.

transcript.name: Transcript
transcript.short: The condensed record of a thread, co-located with its plan set; it serves the audit, while the graph serves the work and the chronicle serves the steering.
transcript.def: A transcript (cb:a520, cb:a540) is the condensed record of a substantive thread, co-located with its plan set, that serves the audit. Every substantive thread persists twice at close - as a transcript and as a chronicle - so the audit trail and the operator narrative are both kept. A decision-weight session persists its thread verbatim, routed by subject.

type-as-function.name: Type as a Function
type-as-function.short: The v2 insight that a belief's structural type is a near-function of its kind-mood, its grounding (artifact vs deps), and its subject scope relative to its deps.
type-as-function.def: In the v2 schema the structural type is close to a deterministic function of three inputs: the mood the kind implies, the grounding (an artifact for a single source versus deps for derived claims), and the subject scope relative to the deps (cb:a472). This is why the kind-type table, the grounding check, and subject containment together can largely decide a belief's type from its other fields, and why the verifier interrogates structure rather than the author's intent.

unlinked-directive.name: Unlinked Directive
unlinked-directive.short: A directive with no materialized items - an open obligation, the structural form of a live todo - and the filter that defines the backlog view.
unlinked-directive.def: An unlinked directive is one with no materialized items: an open obligation, which is the structural form of a live todo (cb:a489). unlinked is a list filter (filter.ex), and combined with the lifecycle:discrete tag it defines the desk. The backlog has its own structural type because a todo is just a directive not yet materialized.

verdict.name: Verdict
verdict.short: The falsifiable eval finding (an inference, kind:verdict) scoped to a model_version; derived never attested or prescribed, and the staleness pivot when a re-run changes the answer.
verdict.def: A verdict is the falsifiable eval finding: an inference with kind: verdict, scoped to a model_version subject (eval-ledger.md). It is derived, never merely attested or prescribed - contract method:c10 requires a verdict to be an inference - and it must be corroborated and cite at least three distinct runs (method:c4, method:c7). It is the staleness pivot: a re-run on a new snapshot supersedes the verdict while the observations beneath it stay true. The worked example is sdl:a008.

verdict-category-firewall.name: Verdict-Category Firewall
verdict-category-firewall.short: The neutrality rule (method:a6) that amieval publishes no verdict in any category where an owner-held product is sold; the self-eval venue rule (method:a7) bars judging the ledger itself.
verdict-category-firewall.def: The verdict-category firewall (method:a6) is the neutrality rule: the eval practice publishes no verdict in any category where an owner-held product is sold, so the evaluator is never a contestant in what it judges (the sold-category list is currently empty). The companion self-eval-venue rule (method:a7) keeps the ledger framework itself from being the system under test in amieval's own published evals. Both are public, citable beliefs, and they are what keep public verdicts receptive-only while generative remediation stays private.

verification-not-generation.name: Verification, Not Generation
verification-not-generation.short: Contracts are never compiled into code; the relationship is verification, so drift surfaces as a failing test rather than a regenerated module.
verification-not-generation.def: Contracts are never compiled into code (cb:a465): spec-to-code generation is nondeterministic and unmaintainable, so the contract-to-code relationship is verification rather than generation - drift surfaces as a failing test, not a regenerated module (the verify_against_contract pattern, cb:a417, cb:a425). This keeps a contract the durable artifact and the code its checked implementation, and it is why the graph can drive tests safely across the routing/implementation boundary.

verify-against-contract.name: verify_against_contract
verify-against-contract.short: The pattern where a module implementing contract-governed behavior self-checks against the contract in the graph, so divergence shows up as a failing test.
verify-against-contract.def: verify_against_contract/0 is the pattern by which a module implementing contract-governed behavior self-checks against the contract that governs it (cb:a417, cb:a425, cb:a416). When the code and the in-graph contract diverge, the divergence shows up as a failing test rather than silently. It is the concrete realization of verification, not generation, and it is exercised by mix cb.verify.schema, which checks the CB.Belief struct against the schema contracts in the graph.

verify-schema.name: verify.schema
verify-schema.short: The task that verifies a collection against the contracts it carries, keeping the CB.Belief struct and the in-graph schema contracts in sync.
verify-schema.def: mix cb.verify.schema runs CB.Schema.Verifier.check/1 over a collection, returning a list of {name, :ok | :fail | :skip, detail} results (verifier.ex:44). It checks framework-universal structure against the CB.Belief canon and collection-specific vocabulary discovered by role, keeping struct and in-graph schema contracts from drifting apart. The companion test/cb/schema_contracts_test.exs asserts the same in the other direction.

write-flow.name: Write Flow
write-flow.short: The sanctioned authoring surface - preflight, then adjudicate, then import - dry-run by default; the graph file is never hand-edited.
write-flow.def: The write flow is the only sanctioned way to author beliefs: preflight a proposal against the live graph, adjudicate any conflicts as a captured decision, then import the result (cb:a533, cb:a387, cb:a381). Every write task is dry-run by default and applies only with --write. The graph file is never hand-edited, because hand-editing would bypass the conflict surfacing and the immutable audit trail that the no maintenance hatch principle depends on. Three narrow front doors handle in-place growth, todo closing, and dep re-pointing. See the write flow.
